proposals.title: A Foundation Model for General Patient Populations: Development and Validation Using Multi-National Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Most clinical foundation models, such as MOTOR, are trained on inpatient cohorts, which introduces significant selection bias and limits generalizability. In real-world healthcare, the vast majority of patient interactions and critical treatment decisions occur in outpatient and emergency settings. A model trained only on hospitalized patients fails to capture the longitudinal health trajectories and treatment effects for the broader population, hindering its utility for preventive care, chronic disease management, and population health analytics.
proposals.hypothesis: A time-to-event foundation model trained on longitudinal sequences from general patient populations (encompassing inpatient, outpatient, and emergency care) will yield more accurate and generalizable predictions of clinical outcomes and treatment effect heterogeneity across diverse care settings compared to models trained solely on inpatient data.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR architecture, a transformer-based model for structured medical records, to process patient timelines from first recorded contact. Using a multi-national network of 26+ databases (similar to prior work), we will construct a cohort spanning all care settings. The model will be pre-trained using a masked event prediction objective on this broad data. Fine-tuning will focus on time-to-event tasks, including the principled estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) for drugs like dabigatran, using methods that account for confounding across the care continuum.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated foundation model for general patient populations, directly addressing the selection bias of inpatient-only models. It will establish new baseline incidence rates for outcomes across care settings and deliver a more robust framework for HTE estimation that is applicable to the point of initial treatment decision, often in outpatient care.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly relaxing the inpatient admission requirement for foundation model training, a major limitation in the field. This shift enables modeling of complete patient journeys from the onset of care, which is non-trivial due to the increased noise, sparsity, and administrative heterogeneity of outpatient data compared to curated inpatient records.

proposals.title: A Comparative Evaluation of Fine-tuning Strategies for Clinical Foundation Models: Full Model Adaptation vs. Frozen Representations for Time-to-Event and Treatment Effect Prediction
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHR), such as MOTOR, offer transformative potential for clinical prediction tasks like time-to-event analysis and treatment effect heterogeneity. However, the optimal strategy for adapting these large, pre-trained models to specific, data-scarce clinical tasks remains unclear. Determining whether to fine-tune the entire model or use frozen representations is critical for deploying robust, performant, and computationally efficient models in real-world healthcare settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for clinical tasks with limited labeled data, fine-tuning only a lightweight head on frozen foundation model representations will achieve comparable or superior performance to full-model fine-tuning, while offering greater computational efficiency and reduced risk of catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained medical knowledge.
proposals.method: We will use the MOTOR foundation model, pre-trained on longitudinal EHR data, as our base. For downstream tasks, we will implement and compare two strategies: (1) full fine-tuning of all MOTOR parameters, and (2) extracting frozen patient representations from MOTOR to train task-specific prediction heads (e.g., Cox proportional hazards models or T-learners for treatment effect estimation). Evaluation will be conducted on established clinical benchmarks, including time-to-event prediction (e.g., heart failure) and estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects (e.g., anticoagulants in atrial fibrillation), using metrics like time-dependent AUC and precision in estimation of heterogeneous effects (PEHE).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, empirical evidence for best practices in adapting EHR foundation models to core clinical prediction tasks. It will deliver a practical framework for researchers and clinicians to efficiently leverage large pre-trained models, balancing performance with computational cost. The findings will directly inform the development of more reliable and deployable clinical AI tools.
proposals.novelty: While fine-tuning strategies are studied in NLP and vision, a rigorous comparison in the context of longitudinal clinical foundation models for causal and survival outcomes is novel. This work uniquely bridges advances in medical foundation models (MOTOR) with established methodologies for treatment effect estimation and time-to-event analysis from Shah's prior work.

proposals.title: The Single-Institution Foundation Model (SIFM): Assessing the Viability and Robustness of Hospital-Specific Pretraining for Clinical Prediction Tasks
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for electronic health records (EHRs), like MOTOR, are typically trained on massive, multi-institutional datasets to ensure broad generalizability. However, this approach obscures institution-specific data structures, clinical practices, and local patient populations, potentially limiting model performance and trust for local clinical decision support. Investigating whether foundation models can be effectively pretrained on the data of a single hospital is crucial for enabling tailored, privacy-preserving AI that aligns with an institution's unique operational context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer-based foundation model, pretrained exclusively on the longitudinal EHR data from a single large academic medical center, will achieve non-inferior performance on local phenotyping and time-to-event prediction tasks compared to a model pretrained on a large multi-institutional cohort, while demonstrating superior calibration and robustness to local data shifts.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MOTOR architecture, a transformer model for structured medical records, for single-institution pretraining. Using a large, de-identified EHR dataset from a single US academic hospital (2015-2024), we will pretrain the model on masked token prediction and next-event prediction tasks. We will benchmark this Single-Institution Foundation Model (SIFM) against a publicly available MOTOR model pretrained on the multi-institutional OHDSI network. Evaluation will focus on 5-10 clinical prediction tasks (e.g., heart failure onset, sepsis) using time-varying Cox models built on the models' embeddings, assessing discrimination (C-index), calibration, and performance across patient subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous evaluation of the feasibility and trade-offs of single-institution clinical foundation models. It will establish benchmarks for local model performance and offer practical guidance for hospitals considering developing their own AI infrastructure. The findings will inform the debate on centralized vs. federated vs. localized model development for healthcare AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on scaling up data for foundation models, this proposal systematically investigates the opposite direction: scaling down to a single source. It directly tests whether the 'foundation' of a generalizable clinical AI can be built from locally sourced data, challenging the prevailing multi-database paradigm.

proposals.title: Data-Centric Diagnostics: Using Foundation Model Robustness to Quantify EHR Data Stability and Informative Content
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for Electronic Health Records (EHRs), like MOTOR, promise robust performance across diverse clinical datasets. However, the inverse question is critical: what does a model's cross-source robustness reveal about the underlying data? Pre-2026, data quality assessment is often manual or rule-based, lacking a systematic, model-driven framework to quantify the inherent stability and clinical informativeness of EHR data streams across institutions and time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the robustness profile of a pre-trained time-to-event foundation model, when evaluated across heterogeneous databases, serves as a sensitive diagnostic for latent data properties, specifically the temporal stability of feature representations and the presence of systematic, informative variation versus non-informative noise.
proposals.method: We will leverage the pre-trained MOTOR model architecture and apply it to a curated set of 4-6 large-scale EHR databases (e.g., from the OHDSI network, similar to prior work). Instead of focusing on predictive performance, we will design a suite of probing tasks: (1) measuring prediction consistency for the same clinical endpoint (e.g., heart failure) under systematic data perturbations (e.g., site-specific code mappings, temporal shifts), and (2) analyzing layer-wise representations via centered kernel alignment (CKA) to quantify feature distribution shifts. We will correlate these model-centric metrics with traditional data-quality measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-based framework for data quality assessment in EHR research, moving beyond simple completeness checks. It will produce quantifiable metrics for 'data stability' and 'informative content' that can guide database selection for clinical studies and inform the design of more generalizable models. The results will offer empirical evidence on which data facets most challenge model robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses models to predict from data, this inverts the paradigm to use a robust model as a lens to diagnose data. It moves from assessing model performance on data to assessing data quality via model behavior, a data-centric AI approach novel to clinical informatics.

proposals.title: A Competing Risks Foundation Model for In-Hospital Mortality: Jointly Modeling Mortality and Discharge to Account for Informative Censoring
proposals.motivation: In-hospital mortality prediction models typically treat discharge as a non-informative censoring event, assuming independence between the risk of death and the likelihood of discharge. This assumption is often violated, as clinical decisions to discharge a patient are directly influenced by their evolving risk of death. Ignoring this correlation can bias model estimates, leading to inaccurate risk assessments and potentially harmful clinical decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling hospital discharge and in-hospital mortality as competing, dependent events using a time-to-event foundation model will yield more accurate and clinically interpretable mortality risk predictions than models assuming independence.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR foundation model architecture to a competing risks framework. The model will ingest structured electronic health record (EHR) data as temporal sequences. We will employ a cause-specific hazards approach, using a shared transformer-based encoder for feature representation, followed by separate output heads for the cause-specific hazard functions of mortality and discharge. Dependence will be captured through the shared latent representation and potentially via a parametric copula model on the cumulative incidence functions. We will train and validate the model on large-scale, multi-hospital EHR databases, similar to those used in Shah's prior multinational studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for in-hospital mortality prediction that accounts for a key source of bias. It will contribute a new open-source, competing risks foundation model for structured medical data. The results will offer clinicians more reliable, dynamic risk estimates that reflect the realistic clinical trade-off between discharge and deterioration.
proposals.novelty: While competing risks models exist in biostatistics, their integration into a large-scale, deep learning foundation model for EHR data is novel. This moves beyond standard single-event time-to-event models (like MOTOR) and simpler classification approaches by directly addressing the non-independent censoring problem inherent in hospital outcomes.

proposals.title: A Preference-Weighted Net Benefit Framework for Optimizing Advance Care Planning Timing and Setting: A Causal Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: The net benefit framework is a cornerstone of clinical decision analysis but often uses population-level utility weights, failing to account for heterogeneous patient preferences. For decisions like Advance Care Planning (ACP) timing and setting, patient values regarding autonomy, burden, and family involvement are critical yet variable. Ignoring this heterogeneity can lead to recommendations misaligned with individual patient priorities, reducing the real-world effectiveness of ACP interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating quantitative, patient-specific preference measures into the net benefit calculation will identify clinically meaningful subgroups for whom the optimal ACP strategy differs from the population-average recommendation, thereby improving the patient-centeredness of decision support.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale electronic health record (EHR) data linked to patient preference surveys on ACP attributes. Using a time-to-event foundation model (e.g., an extension of the MOTOR architecture) trained on structured EHRs, we will estimate individualized risks of clinical deterioration. We will then apply a causal forest or heterogeneous treatment effect (HTE) estimation method, as in Shah's prior work on dabigatran, to model the interaction between treatment (e.g., early vs. standard ACP initiation), patient covariates, and quantified preference scores. The net benefit for each patient will be calculated using their personal preference weights as utilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable methodology for integrating patient preferences into quantitative decision models using real-world data. It will deliver a proof-of-concept for personalized ACP timing recommendations and a generalizable framework for preference-weighted net benefit analysis applicable to other preference-sensitive care decisions.
proposals.novelty: While HTE analysis is established, its integration with formal, quantitative patient preference data within a net benefit framework is novel. This moves beyond identifying 'who benefits more' clinically to 'who values the benefit more,' a crucial distinction for patient-centered care.

proposals.title: A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Dynamic Triage of Advanced Care Planning in High-Volume Clinical Settings
proposals.motivation: Advanced Care Planning (ACP) is crucial for patient-centered care but is often underutilized due to limited clinician capacity and difficulty identifying patients who would benefit most at a given time. Current static risk models fail to adapt to dynamic clinic workflows, fluctuating resource availability, and evolving patient states, leading to inefficient prioritization and missed opportunities for timely conversations. An adaptive, data-driven triage system is needed to optimize ACP engagement within real-world operational constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning (RL) agent, trained on longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) data and operational logs, can learn a dynamic triage policy that maximizes long-term, value-sensitive patient engagement with ACP services under variable clinical capacity, outperforming static rule-based or prediction-only approaches.
proposals.method: We will frame dynamic ACP triage as a Markov Decision Process. The state space will integrate time-to-event foundation model outputs (e.g., MOTOR) for patient trajectory predictions, real-time operational metrics (e.g., staff capacity), and patient ACP status. The action space will be a priority score assignment. We will employ an offline RL algorithm, such as Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), to learn from historical EHR and workflow data, ensuring safety by mitigating distributional shift. The reward function will balance clinical urgency (e.g., predicted proximity to high-acuity events) with conversation feasibility and patient preferences inferred from past care patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel operational AI framework that directly integrates predictive clinical intelligence with resource-aware decision-making for workflow management. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and potential impact of RL for real-time patient prioritization in a complex, low-frequency intervention setting like ACP. The resulting policy and evaluation methodology will offer a template for dynamic triage of other preventive or time-sensitive care processes.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been explored for static treatment scheduling, its application to dynamic, capacity-constrained triage for a longitudinal communicative intervention like ACP is novel. This approach moves beyond predicting 'who' needs ACP to algorithmically deciding 'when to act' given evolving patient and system states, a gap not addressed by prior time-to-event or heterogeneity-of-treatment-effect models.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Risk-Stratified Intervention for Scaling Advance Care Planning Engagement in Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Advance Care Planning (ACP) is crucial for patient-centered end-of-life care but is underutilized, partly due to clinician alert fatigue from non-discriminative prompts. Pre-2026, machine learning models for mortality prediction have matured, yet their integration into scalable, tiered clinical workflows to optimize ACP discussions remains unproven. This creates a missed opportunity to use electronic health record (EHR) data to prompt the right conversation, with the right intensity, for the right patient at the right time.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-level intervention where the modality and intensity of the ACP prompt (e.g., passive flag vs. active alert vs. team notification) scales with a patient's predicted short-term mortality probability will increase appropriate ACP documentation rates without increasing clinician burden, compared to a non-stratified, one-size-fits-all prompt.
proposals.method: We will develop a time-to-event foundation model (e.g., extending MOTOR architecture) on structured EHR data from a large network (e.g., 24+ million patients across multiple databases) to predict 6- and 12-month mortality. We will define risk tiers (e.g., low, medium, high) and map them to distinct EHR-integrated prompts of increasing intensity. Using a stepped-wedge cluster randomized design, we will evaluate the intervention's effect on ACP documentation rates and clinician satisfaction, adjusting for confounders via methods for principled treatment effect heterogeneity estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable framework for implementing risk-based clinical decision support, moving beyond binary alerts. It will generate evidence on how to effectively translate probabilistic model outputs into differentiated clinical actions, specifically for improving goal-concordant care. The study will also offer insights into the real-world performance and integration challenges of time-to-event foundation models in pragmatic trials.
proposals.novelty: While risk prediction models exist, the novel integration lies in dynamically scaling a behavioral intervention's intensity based on a continuous model output and rigorously testing it in a real-world clinical workflow. This bridges the gap between predictive analytics and implementation science for ACP, a domain where one-size-fits-all digital prompts have historically failed.

proposals.title: Generalizable vs. Disease-Specific AI for Respiratory Distress: A Multinational Cohort Study on Model Performance for Novel Pathogens
proposals.motivation: The emergence of novel respiratory pathogens like SARS-CoV-2 exposed a critical weakness in clinical AI: models trained on specific diseases (e.g., bacterial pneumonia) often fail when faced with new etiologies. Developing bespoke models for each novel threat is slow and resource-intensive, delaying clinical decision support during pandemics. A generalizable AI for the syndromic presentation of respiratory distress could provide immediate utility for triage and prognosis, irrespective of the causative pathogen.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model trained on time-to-event outcomes from heterogeneous, multi-database electronic health record (EHR) data for general respiratory distress will outperform disease-specific models in predicting clinical deterioration (e.g., mechanical ventilation, mortality) in patients infected with a novel respiratory pathogen not seen during training.
proposals.method: We will leverage the MOTOR foundation model architecture, a transformer-based time-to-event model pre-trained on structured EHR data, adapting it for respiratory distress. Using a multinational network of 15+ databases (similar to the 26-database study in Shah's prior work), we will train a general model on all-cause respiratory distress episodes pre-2020. We will fine-tune disease-specific models (e.g., for influenza, bacterial pneumonia) on respective subsets. Performance will be evaluated on a held-out cohort of COVID-19 patients from early 2020, treating it as a novel pathogen, assessing calibration and discrimination for 7-day deterioration risk.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, empirical evidence on the trade-offs between generalizable and disease-specific AI for acute syndromic presentations. It will deliver a validated, deployable foundation model for respiratory distress risk stratification that is robust to pathogen shifts. The findings will inform health system preparedness by establishing a paradigm for 'out-of-distribution' clinical AI evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While disease-specific models dominate, no prior study has rigorously benchmarked a general-purpose respiratory AI against a suite of specific models using a real-world, novel pathogen as the test case. The application of a time-to-event foundation model (MOTOR) to this problem, evaluated across disparate international databases, is a novel synthesis of Shah's methodological expertise.

proposals.title: CURATE: A Framework for Curation, Temporal Alignment, and De-identification of Multi-database Electronic Health Records for Foundation Model Training
proposals.motivation: The development of robust medical large language models (LLMs) is hampered by the lack of high-quality, representative, and temporally coherent training datasets. Current efforts often rely on narrow, single-institution data or public benchmarks lacking the scale and clinical realism needed for models to learn nuanced patient trajectories and treatment effects. This limits the utility and safety of medical LLMs for clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: A principled, automated framework for curating and harmonizing longitudinal electronic health record (EHR) data from multiple, heterogeneous databases can produce a training corpus that significantly improves the clinical reasoning and temporal understanding of medical foundation models compared to models trained on existing benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR foundation model architecture and its time-to-event pretraining objective. The curation pipeline will implement: 1) Schema mapping and clinical concept normalization using the OMOP Common Data Model, informed by our prior multinational cohort studies. 2) A novel temporal alignment module that uses learned representations to resolve inconsistencies in event timestamps across sources. 3) A differential privacy-preserving synthesis step for de-identification, building on methods from our treatment effect heterogeneity work. The resulting dataset will be used to pretrain a MOTOR-based LLM, evaluated on tasks requiring temporal reasoning (e.g., predicting next visit diagnosis) and treatment effect estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a publicly described, reproducible framework for creating large-scale, multi-source medical pretraining datasets, along with a new open benchmark for evaluating clinical temporal reasoning in LLMs. It will demonstrate how principled data curation directly impacts model performance on tasks critical for patient care.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created datasets or models, our approach uniquely integrates multinational database harmonization experience with a state-of-the-art temporal foundation model (MOTOR) and a focus on solving the specific temporal alignment and privacy challenges inherent in multi-source EHR curation for LLM training.

proposals.title: Interpretable Counterfactual Reasoning for Treatment Effect Heterogeneity: A Framework for Clinical Understanding
proposals.motivation: While high-performing predictive models like MOTOR exist, they often function as black boxes, limiting clinical trust and actionable insight. Understanding *why* a treatment effect varies for a specific patient is as critical as predicting the outcome itself for personalized care. This gap is evident in studies of treatment heterogeneity, like the dabigatran case study, where clinicians need interpretable explanations of differential risk.
proposals.hypothesis: A model that explicitly generates interpretable, counterfactual reasoning pathways from longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs) can elucidate clinically valid drivers of treatment effect heterogeneity with minimal loss in predictive accuracy.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR time-to-event foundation model architecture by integrating a structured attention mechanism and a causal pathway decoder. The model will be trained on large-scale, multinational EHR data (e.g., from the 24-million-patient COVID-19 study network) to predict outcomes under different treatment arms. The decoder will output human-interpretable sequences of clinical states (e.g., "renal function decline -> increased bleed risk") that explain the predicted differential effect for an individual, using techniques from algorithmic recourse and symbolic knowledge distillation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for generating model-based, patient-specific clinical narratives for treatment decisions, moving beyond aggregate heterogeneity measures. It will deliver both a validated methodological toolkit and a clinical evaluation of the generated explanations' plausibility and utility with domain experts.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior interpretability methods applied post-hoc to black-box models, we bake counterfactual clinical reasoning directly into the foundation model's objective. This inverts the standard focus by prioritizing the *explanation pathway* as a primary output, not just a secondary feature attribution.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Real-World Clinical Workflow Utility of a Time-to-Event Foundation Model for In-Hospital Deterioration and Resource Use
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHRs), such as MOTOR, show promise on academic benchmarks but their utility for direct patient care and operational decision-making in real-world clinical settings remains unproven. Evaluating these models on tasks that mirror actual clinical workflows—like predicting near-term patient deterioration or forecasting short-term bed demand—is critical for translating AI research into tangible improvements in care quality and hospital efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: A time-to-event foundation model, fine-tuned on multi-hospital EHR data, will demonstrate superior performance and operational feasibility over traditional task-specific models for real-time clinical prediction tasks that directly influence bedside care and resource allocation decisions.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MOTOR architecture, a transformer-based foundation model for temporal structured data, and fine-tune it using continuous pre-training and task-specific heads on a multinational dataset of 5-10 million hospital encounters. The evaluation will focus on two workflow-relevant tasks: 1) real-time prediction of a composite outcome of ICU transfer or death within 24 hours, and 2) forecasting ward-level bed occupancy 48 hours ahead. Performance will be compared against established baselines (e.g., logistic regression, gradient boosting, RNNs) using both discrimination metrics (e.g., time-dependent AUC) and operational metrics (e.g., alert burden, resource planning error).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, clinically grounded framework for assessing the real-world value of medical foundation models, moving beyond static benchmarks. It will deliver concrete evidence on whether such models can be integrated into live clinical systems to improve prognostication and operational planning, directly addressing the translational gap in medical AI.
proposals.novelty: This study shifts the evaluation paradigm from retrospective benchmark accuracy to prospective workflow utility, assessing foundation models on dynamic, operationally critical tasks that require continuous, real-time inference—a significant departure from most prior validation studies.

proposals.title: A Multi-Task Benchmarking Study of Foundation Model Architectures for Structured Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHR), such as MOTOR, has created a fragmented landscape where architectural choices (e.g., transformers, RNNs, temporal point processes) are often justified on single tasks or datasets. There is a critical lack of systematic, head-to-head comparisons across a standardized suite of core clinical prediction tasks, hindering evidence-based model selection for clinical decision support and research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative performance of different temporal foundation model architectures is not consistent across diverse clinical tasks (e.g., time-to-event, classification, phenotyping), and that a task-informed architecture selection framework will significantly outperform a one-size-fits-all approach.
proposals.method: We will conduct a rigorous benchmarking study using large-scale, multi-source EHR data (e.g., from the OHDSI network, pre-2026). We will train and evaluate three distinct architectural families—transformer-based (e.g., MOTOR), advanced RNNs (e.g., GRU-D), and neural temporal point processes—on a standardized evaluation suite of 5-8 tasks. Tasks will span time-to-event (inspired by the dabigatran study), longitudinal classification, and baseline rate characterization (inspired by the AE study). Hyperparameter optimization and training will be controlled via a unified pipeline, with performance assessed on held-out data across multiple clinical sites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, multi-task benchmark for EHR foundation models, yielding actionable guidelines for architecture selection based on task requirements. It will produce an open-source evaluation framework to standardize future model comparisons and advance reproducible clinical AI research.
proposals.novelty: While individual models are benchmarked, a controlled, multi-architecture comparison across a comprehensive suite of tasks reflecting real-world clinical heterogeneity is novel. The focus on deriving a practical selection framework, rather than proposing a single new model, distinguishes this work.

proposals.title: A Framework for Defining and Validating High-Impact Use Cases for Electronic Health Record Foundation Models in Clinical Risk Prediction
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHRs), like MOTOR, represent a paradigm shift in clinical analytics, but their practical utility remains ambiguous. Without a clear articulation of specific, high-value applications, their adoption in clinical care and research will be hindered by uncertainty about where they offer unique advantages over well-established, interpretable traditional methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EHR foundation models provide unique, measurable value in clinical prediction tasks characterized by complex, longitudinal patient trajectories and heterogeneous treatment effects, where they will outperform traditional models in calibration and discrimination for specific, pre-defined endpoints.
proposals.method: We will define candidate use cases through a structured Delphi process with clinical domain experts, focusing on time-to-event prediction. Using the MOTOR architecture as a base, we will fine-tune and evaluate the model on these specific tasks within a large-scale, multi-database network (e.g., Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics [OHDSI] framework). Performance will be benchmarked against state-of-the-art traditional methods (e.g., penalized Cox models, gradient-boosted survival models) using metrics for discrimination (e.g., time-dependent AUC) and calibration, with a focus on heterogeneous subpopulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated framework and a prioritized list of clinically impactful use cases for EHR foundation models, moving beyond generic claims of superiority. It will provide empirical evidence of where these models uniquely excel, guiding future development and implementation efforts in both academic and operational settings.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has developed foundation models or applied ML to specific tasks, this proposal systematically defines and validates their *unique* value proposition for a set of high-stakes clinical predictions, directly addressing the translational gap between model capability and demonstrated clinical utility.

proposals.title: Causal Evaluation of a Time-to-Event Foundation Model's Impact on Anticoagulant Treatment Decisions and Patient Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHRs), like MOTOR, show strong associative performance for tasks like risk prediction. However, their real-world clinical impact on downstream treatment decisions and patient outcomes remains unproven. A causal evaluation is urgently needed to move beyond predictive accuracy and assess whether model-guided care actually improves outcomes like stroke prevention or reduces major bleeding, especially for high-stakes decisions such as anticoagulant prescribing in atrial fibrillation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treatment recommendations informed by a time-to-event foundation model (MOTOR), when framed within a causal inference paradigm, will lead to a measurable reduction in the composite outcome of ischemic stroke and major bleeding compared to standard care, by better identifying heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE) for drugs like dabigatran.
proposals.method: We will use a multinational retrospective cohort (e.g., from the OHDSI network) of AF patients. We will emulate a target trial comparing model-guided vs. standard care. The MOTOR model will be used to estimate per-patient conditional average treatment effects (CATE) for dabigatran vs. warfarin. We will apply advanced causal methods, specifically double/debiased machine learning and propensity score-based g-computation, to estimate the average treatment effect of following the model's recommendation on the time-to-event composite outcome, rigorously adjusting for time-varying confounding captured in the longitudinal EHR.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, causal framework for the clinical evaluation of EHR foundation models, shifting the focus from prediction to intervention impact. It will deliver an empirical estimate of the potential benefit (or harm) of deploying MOTOR for a critical treatment decision, setting a new methodological standard for model evaluation in healthcare.
proposals.novelty: While Shah et al. have developed MOTOR and studied HTE for dabigatran separately, this proposal novelly integrates these strands by using the foundation model to estimate CATE and then applying causal inference to evaluate the model's *own* effect as a clinical intervention, a significant advance over typical associative validation.

proposals.title: MED-LoRA: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Foundation Models for Real-Time Clinical Risk Prediction on Edge Hardware
proposals.motivation: Large clinical foundation models like MOTOR enable powerful temporal analysis but require significant computational resources, limiting their deployment in real-time hospital settings such as the ICU or operating room. Pre-2026, there is a critical gap between the performance of these models and the hardware constraints of clinical environments, which impedes the translation of AI research into point-of-care tools that can augment clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying advanced parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques, specifically Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and its variants, to a pre-trained clinical time-to-event foundation model, we can create ultra-efficient specialized models that retain >95% of the predictive performance of a fully fine-tuned model while reducing the computational footprint by over 90%, enabling real-time inference on hospital-grade edge devices.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune the MOTOR foundation model using structured EHR data from the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership (OMOP) Common Data Model. The core technical approach will implement and compare multiple PEFT strategies: standard LoRA, AdaLoRA (which adaptively allocates rank budget), and a novel task-adaptive pruning method applied to LoRA modules. We will evaluate these models on time-to-event prediction tasks (e.g., in-hospital deterioration, acute kidney injury) and benchmark their inference latency, memory usage, and predictive accuracy (C-index, Brier score) against a fully fine-tuned baseline on representative edge hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source framework for creating ultra-efficient clinical risk models, directly addressing the deployment bottleneck. It will provide empirical evidence on the performance-efficiency trade-offs of different PEFT methods in the clinical domain and produce benchmark models ready for pilot integration in hospital workflows.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT methods like LoRA are established in NLP, their systematic application and optimization for clinical foundation models—particularly for temporal, structured EHR data and time-to-event outcomes—remains unexplored. This project uniquely focuses on the intersection of efficiency techniques and real-world clinical deployment constraints, moving beyond mere algorithmic performance.

proposals.title: DUAL: A Foundation Model for Temporal Anomaly Detection and Audit in Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Electronic health records (EHRs) are foundational for clinical research and care, but they contain pervasive errors, biases, and inconsistencies that compromise data quality and downstream predictive models. Existing methods focus on prediction using potentially flawed data, rather than systematically auditing the data-generating process itself. A model capable of detecting temporal and logical anomalies in EHRs at scale would improve data trustworthiness and patient safety.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model trained on the temporal sequences of structured EHR data can learn the expected clinical workflow patterns and, by identifying significant deviations, reliably flag data entry errors, protocol violations, and potential biases in treatment pathways.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MOTOR time-to-event foundation model architecture, pre-trained on large-scale, multi-database EHR data, to perform anomaly detection. The model will be fine-tuned to output an anomaly score for each clinical event (e.g., medication order, lab test) based on its likelihood given the patient's prior history and population-level patterns. We will employ contrastive learning to distinguish between true clinical outliers and rare but valid events, and validate detected anomalies against manual chart reviews in a subset of the 26-database network used in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable tool for automated EHR data quality auditing, moving beyond descriptive summaries to temporal-context-aware anomaly detection. It will contribute a new benchmark and evaluation framework for assessing data integrity in observational health research and clinical operations.
proposals.novelty: While foundation models like MOTOR exist for prediction, repurposing them as auditors of the data-generating process is a novel paradigm shift. The approach moves from modeling outcomes given data to modeling the plausibility of the data itself within its clinical context.

proposals.title: CLIP-EHR: Aligning Structured Patient Histories with Clinical Narratives via Contrastive Pre-training
proposals.motivation: Electronic Health Records (EHRs) contain rich, complementary data modalities: structured codes (diagnoses, medications) and unstructured clinical notes. Current models often treat these separately, failing to capture their inherent alignment, which limits the development of robust, multimodal foundation models for healthcare. A method to learn a unified representation space from these modalities, analogous to CLIP for vision-language, is needed to improve downstream tasks like patient phenotyping and outcome prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a contrastive learning objective, adapted from the CLIP framework, can learn semantically aligned representations of structured EHR sequences and their corresponding clinical text, resulting in a foundation model that outperforms modality-specific or late-fusion models on key clinical prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CLIP architecture, treating structured EHR data (modeled as temporal sequences of codes using a transformer encoder like in MOTOR) as one modality and clinical note embeddings (from a pre-trained clinical BERT) as the other. A contrastive loss (InfoNCE) will be used to maximize the similarity of representations from the same patient/time period while minimizing similarity for mismatched pairs. The model will be pre-trained on a large-scale, de-identified EHR database (e.g., Optum or a UK Biobank-like cohort), then evaluated on time-to-event prediction (e.g., heart failure) and phenotype classification tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, publicly released pre-trained model (CLIP-EHR) that learns aligned multimodal EHR representations, 2) a framework for applying vision-language techniques to structured-temporal data, and 3) empirical evidence on the value of modality alignment for improving robustness and performance in clinical prediction models, directly extending Shah's work on time-to-event models and treatment effect heterogeneity.
proposals.novelty: While CLIP is established for images and text, its application to align structured, temporal EHR data with clinical narratives is novel. This moves beyond simple multimodal fusion to principled, self-supervised alignment of fundamentally different data structures (discrete time series vs. free text) within the clinical domain.

proposals.title: Graph Neural Networks for Patient Cohort Representation to Enhance Few-Shot Learning of Treatment Outcomes
proposals.motivation: In clinical research, predicting outcomes for rare conditions or novel treatments is hampered by limited patient data, a classic few-shot learning challenge. Existing methods like MOTOR leverage temporal sequences but often treat patients as independent, ignoring the rich relational structure inherent in multi-morbidity patterns across a population. A method to exploit these shared-disease relationships could significantly improve model performance in data-scarce scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a patient graph based on shared clinical conditions and applying a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to learn patient representations will capture latent epidemiological structure, leading to more robust and generalizable predictions in few-shot learning tasks for treatment effect estimation.
proposals.method: We will construct an undirected patient graph from a large-scale electronic health record database (e.g., the 24-million-patient network from Shah's prior work), where nodes are patients and edges are weighted by similarity of diagnosed conditions. We will employ a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) or GraphSAGE architecture to generate node embeddings. These embeddings, alongside temporal features from a model like MOTOR, will serve as input to a downstream few-shot meta-learner (e.g., Prototypical Networks) tasked with predicting individualized treatment effects for under-represented patient subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, structured deep learning framework that integrates relational patient information for few-shot clinical prediction. It will provide a principled method, extending Shah's work on treatment effect heterogeneity, to estimate outcomes for rare patient phenotypes, thereby improving the feasibility of personalized medicine in low-data regimes.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs have been used for drug discovery or disease prediction, their application to model patient-patient relationships for few-shot treatment effect estimation is novel. This moves beyond modeling patients in isolation or within simple cohorts to explicitly learning from the topology of a population-scale disease co-occurrence network.

proposals.title: EdgeMED: Ultra-Efficient Clinical Time-to-Event Models via Task-Aware Distillation of a Medical Foundation Model
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models like MOTOR enable powerful temporal predictions from electronic health records, but their size precludes deployment on resource-constrained edge devices in clinics and at the point of care. This creates a barrier to real-time, privacy-preserving clinical decision support. An ultra-efficient model is needed to bring advanced predictive analytics directly to clinical workflows without reliance on cloud connectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: A large, pre-trained clinical foundation model can be effectively distilled into a task-specific, ultra-compact model (<10M parameters) that retains robust performance for key time-to-event predictions, enabling accurate, on-device inference.
proposals.method: We will use the MOTOR foundation model as the teacher. For distillation, we will employ a combination of response-based and feature-based knowledge distillation. The student architecture will be a compact, temporal transformer with efficient attention mechanisms (e.g., Linformer or Performer). We will train the student on task-specific data (e.g., time-to-readmission, time-to-treatment-change) using a multi-objective loss combining the standard task loss with distillation losses (KL divergence on logits and mean squared error on intermediate layer features).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, deployable model architecture for edge clinical computing and a validated framework for distilling large-scale medical foundation models into efficient variants. It will provide empirical evidence on the performance-efficiency trade-off for critical clinical prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: While model distillation is known, its application to large-scale *clinical time-to-event* foundation models for edge deployment is unexplored. The novelty lies in the specific adaptation of distillation techniques to preserve the nuanced temporal dependencies and censoring-aware learning inherent in models like MOTOR.

proposals.title: A Comparative Evaluation of General-Purpose vs. Clinically-Tuned Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support: A Time-to-Event and Treatment Effect Heterogeneity Analysis
proposals.motivation: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) in healthcare creates a critical, unresolved tension: should institutions deploy powerful, general-purpose models (e.g., GPT-4) or invest in models specifically tuned on clinical data (e.g., ClinicalBERT, BioGPT)? Pre-2026 evidence is fragmented, lacking rigorous, patient-centered evaluation on core clinical tasks like risk prediction and treatment effect estimation, where safety and accuracy are paramount. This gap hinders evidence-based policy for AI integration into clinical workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while clinically-tuned models will demonstrate superior performance on structured clinical reasoning tasks (e.g., time-to-event prediction), general-purpose LLMs will exhibit competitive or superior performance on tasks requiring broader medical knowledge synthesis and patient communication, provided they are augmented with appropriate retrieval mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the MOTOR foundation model framework to generate patient representations from structured EHR data across multiple databases. We will benchmark general-purpose LLMs (GPT-4, LLaMA-2) against clinically-tuned models (ClinicalBERT, BioGPT) on two core tasks: 1) Time-to-event prediction for clinical outcomes (extending MOTOR's methodology), and 2) Estimation of heterogeneous treatment effects for a standard intervention (e.g., anticoagulants in AFib), using the principled evaluation framework from Shah's prior work. Models will be prompted or fine-tuned in a consistent manner, with performance assessed via calibration, discrimination, and clinical utility metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, multi-task, and multi-database evidence base for the strategic selection of LLMs in clinical support. It will yield a methodological framework for the rigorous evaluation of generative AI in healthcare and produce actionable guidelines for health systems on whether and when to use general-purpose versus specialized models.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond benchmarking simple QA tasks to evaluate LLMs on core, high-stakes clinical predictive tasks using established causal and time-to-event frameworks. It uniquely integrates the evaluation of treatment effect heterogeneity—a critical aspect of personalized medicine—into the LLM comparison, which is absent from pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: SYNTHESIS: A Synthetic Patient Generation Framework for Scaling and Enhancing Time-to-Event Foundation Model Training
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHRs), like MOTOR, require massive, diverse longitudinal cohorts to robustly capture disease progression and treatment effects. However, real-world data is often siloed, limited in size for rare outcomes, and constrained by privacy regulations, hindering model generalizability and the study of treatment effect heterogeneity. Generating high-fidelity, privacy-preserving synthetic patient trajectories can overcome these data scarcity and sharing bottlenecks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model trained on multi-database EHR cohorts can produce scalable, high-utility synthetic patient data that, when used to augment real data, will significantly improve the performance and calibration of downstream time-to-event models for predicting clinical outcomes and estimating heterogeneous treatment effects.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR architecture into a generative framework. Building on pre-2026 methods like TimeGAN and conditional variational autoencoders for sequences, we will develop a transformer-based generator conditioned on patient demographics and initial diagnoses. The model will be trained on the multinational 24-million-patient cohort from Shah's prior work to learn the joint distribution of longitudinal medical events. We will implement rigorous evaluation using privacy metrics (e.g., membership inference attacks) and utility assessments, measuring the performance of a time-to-event model (trained on synthetic-augmented data) on held-out real data for tasks like stroke prediction in atrial fibrillation patients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source framework for generating privacy-compliant synthetic longitudinal EHRs. It will demonstrate a concrete method to amplify training data for clinical foundation models, enabling more powerful studies of treatment effect heterogeneity and rare adverse events. The resulting synthetic data resource will facilitate broader, safer collaboration in clinical AI research.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic data generation exists, its application to massively scale training for *time-to-event foundation models* is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates Shah's expertise in multinational EHR analysis, treatment effect heterogeneity, and the MOTOR architecture to address the specific data-scale challenges of clinical prognostic modeling.

proposals.title: Auditing and Mitigating Subgroup Performance Disparities in Clinical Foundation Models via Threshold Selection
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (FMs) like MOTOR, trained on massive-scale electronic health records, promise to revolutionize predictive medicine. However, pre-2026 research in non-clinical FMs has shown they can encode and amplify societal biases, leading to significant performance disparities across patient subgroups defined by race, gender, or age. Ensuring these models perform equitably across diverse populations is a critical, unresolved challenge for their safe clinical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a principled, subgroup-specific threshold selection paradigm, applied to the risk scores of a multi-task clinical FM, can significantly mitigate performance disparities (e.g., in AUC-PR, F1) across protected subgroups without requiring model retraining.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a pre-existing clinical time-to-event FM (e.g., MOTOR architecture) on multiple prediction tasks (e.g., heart failure, renal decline). For each task, we will audit performance across predefined subgroups. We will then implement and test fairness-aware threshold selection methods, such as equalized odds post-processing or subgroup-specific threshold optimization, to adjust decision thresholds per subgroup. This will be benchmarked against a naive single-threshold baseline using real-world EHR data from a network like the OHDSI consortium.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, practical framework for auditing and improving fairness in clinical FMs, moving beyond simple aggregate metrics. It will yield a methodological blueprint for deploying FMs with equitable performance and contribute empirical evidence on the nature of bias in multi-task clinical predictive models.
proposals.novelty: While threshold adjustment for fairness is known in simple classifiers, its application and evaluation in the context of large, multi-task temporal clinical foundation models—which have complex, shared representations—is novel and addresses a pressing gap at the intersection of clinical AI and algorithmic fairness.

proposals.title: A Framework for Dynamic Model Updating: Determining Optimal Schedules and Triggers for Clinical Prediction Models
proposals.motivation: The deployment of machine learning models in clinical practice, such as those for treatment effect heterogeneity or adverse event prediction, is undermined by model decay as patient populations, care protocols, and data-generating processes evolve over time. Current practice for updating models is ad hoc, lacking evidence-based guidelines on when to retrain, which risks degraded performance and patient harm. This gap is critical as healthcare systems increasingly rely on electronic health record-based predictive tools for real-time decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimal updating schedules for clinical prediction models can be systematically determined by modeling performance decay as a function of temporal dataset shift and that event-triggered updates based on principled monitoring of performance metrics will be more efficient than periodic retraining.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, multinational electronic health record databases (e.g., from the OHDSI network) to train time-to-event and treatment effect models (extending methods like MOTOR). We will simulate real-world deployment by sequentially revealing data in time. We will evaluate fixed-interval updating against dynamic triggers based on statistical process control charts monitoring performance on recent data and measures of covariate shift (e.g., using Maximum Mean Discrepancy). The core technical approach will frame the update decision as an optimal stopping problem, using reinforcement learning to learn a policy that balances retraining cost against performance loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, data-driven framework for determining model updating frequency in clinical settings, moving beyond expert opinion. It will deliver concrete, generalizable guidelines—both schedule-based and trigger-based—that can be implemented in health system IT infrastructure to maintain model reliability.
proposals.novelty: While model monitoring is studied, the explicit formulation of an optimal updating policy for clinical models, integrating temporal performance decay with real-world operational constraints, is a novel contribution. This bridges the gap between methodological research on temporal validity and practical clinical deployment.

proposals.title: Beyond the Manual: Quantifying the Influence of Peer Networks and Clinical Experience on Clinician Adoption and Understanding of Predictive Models in Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: The successful deployment of clinical prediction models in EHRs depends on clinician trust and appropriate use, which is assumed to be facilitated by formal documentation. However, anecdotal evidence suggests clinicians often rely on informal peer networks and direct experience. Understanding these alternative knowledge pathways is critical for designing effective implementation strategies and ensuring model safety, especially for high-stakes applications like treatment effect heterogeneity or adverse event monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: Clinicians' functional knowledge and adoption patterns of embedded predictive models are more strongly associated with peer network influence and hands-on clinical experience than with engagement with formal model documentation.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-modal, time-to-event study using structured EHR audit logs and unstructured clinical notes from a large healthcare system. Using methods from Shah's MOTOR foundation model, we will temporally encode clinician interactions with model outputs (e.g., alerts, risk scores). We will apply causal inference techniques for treatment effect heterogeneity to estimate the differential effect of documentation access (exposure) versus peer co-usage patterns (inferred via network analysis of co-occurring model accesses) on the subsequent appropriate use of the model, treating time-to-correct application as the primary outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative evidence of how clinician learning about AI models diffuses through clinical practice, moving beyond surveys. It will yield a novel framework for auditing model implementation success and generate actionable insights for health system leaders on whether to invest in documentation or structured peer-to-peer training programs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies model performance or clinician attitudes, this research uniquely treats model understanding as a time-to-event outcome and applies causal and network analysis methods from computational epidemiology to the problem of AI implementation science.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the Real-World Robustness of Multimodal Clinical AI: A Framework for Evaluating Performance Under Distribution Shifts in Electronic Health Records
proposals.motivation: Multimodal foundation models trained on large-scale electronic health record (EHR) data promise to revolutionize clinical decision support. However, their performance is typically validated on held-out test sets from the same data distribution used for training, which does not reflect real-world deployment where models encounter systematic distribution shifts in patient populations, clinical practices, and data coding. This gap risks deploying models that fail silently in new clinical settings, potentially harming patient care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art multimodal clinical AI models, despite high performance on internal benchmarks, will demonstrate significant and clinically meaningful degradation in predictive accuracy when evaluated under realistic, pre-specified distribution shifts encountered in external clinical practice.
proposals.method: We will leverage the MOTOR time-to-event foundation model architecture as a core benchmark. Using a multinational network of EHR databases (e.g., from the OHDSI/OMOP common data model, as in Shah's prior work), we will design a principled evaluation framework. This involves training models on data from one set of healthcare systems and then testing them on external systems, while explicitly characterizing shifts in patient demographics, treatment patterns, and lab value distributions. We will adapt methods from treatment effect heterogeneity analysis to quantify performance variation across subgroups and settings, and employ domain adaptation techniques (e.g., adversarial domain-invariant learning) to test robustness improvements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark of multimodal clinical AI robustness under real-world distribution shifts, offering a validated evaluation framework for the field. It will yield concrete evidence on failure modes of current models and establish methodological best practices for assessing generalizability before clinical deployment, directly informing safer implementation.
proposals.novelty: While distribution shift is a known problem in machine learning, this proposal novelly applies a principled, large-scale epidemiological cohort framework—inspired by the researcher's prior work on multinational baseline rates and treatment heterogeneity—to benchmark modern multimodal foundation models, moving beyond simple train-test splits to clinically-grounded shift simulation.

proposals.title: SAFEGATE: A Robust, Calibrated Foundation Model for Competing Risks with Guaranteed Fallback to Marginal Models
proposals.motivation: Clinical prediction models for time-to-event outcomes, like those for treatment safety or disease progression, must handle competing and correlated risks (e.g., stroke vs. bleeding on anticoagulants). Existing joint models can improve accuracy by capturing dependencies but risk severe miscalibration under model misspecification or data shifts, potentially leading to unsafe clinical decisions. There is a critical need for a framework that provides the benefits of multivariate modeling while guaranteeing a safe baseline performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A gated, multi-event foundation model that dynamically switches between a flexible joint estimator and independent marginal models—based on real-time calibration and domain-shift diagnostics—will achieve predictive performance that is never worse than using marginal models alone, while robustly capturing dependencies when supported by reliable data.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR foundation model architecture to model multiple events using a transformer with cause-specific decoders. Nonparametric copula methods will model dependency structures. A key innovation is a gating module that continuously evaluates performance using metrics like Expected Calibration Error for survival models and measures of covariate shift (e.g., using kernel mean matching). This gate will trigger a fallback to independently trained cause-specific Cox or DeepHit models when joint model reliability drops below a pre-specified safety threshold, ensuring the 'no-harm' guarantee.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a clinically deployable, robust framework for multi-event prediction with a formal safety guarantee. It will provide a principled method for trading off model complexity and reliability in dynamic healthcare settings. The resulting software and validation protocol will directly address concerns about AI safety and reliability in high-stakes medical decision support.
proposals.novelty: While robust or fallback models exist in classification, applying a formal 'no-harm' guarantee and a dynamic gating mechanism to the domain of competing risks and time-to-event foundation models is novel. The integration of continuous calibration monitoring into the model's operational logic for safe fallback represents a significant advance over static ensemble methods.

proposals.title: CLUE-V: A Clinically Grounded, Uncertainty-Aware Explanation and Validation Framework for Time-to-Event Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for time-to-event analysis, like MOTOR, offer powerful predictions but lack clinically credible and actionable explanations. Post-hoc interpretability methods (e.g., SHAP) can produce features that are statistically faithful but clinically implausible or contradictory to established medical knowledge, eroding trust and hindering safe integration into patient care workflows. There is a critical need for explanation systems that are not only faithful to the model but also explicitly validated against clinical knowledge and equipped to communicate prediction uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a knowledge-grounded validation layer with interactive counterfactual probing and explicit uncertainty quantification will generate explanations for time-to-event predictions that are significantly more clinically credible and actionable than standard post-hoc methods, as measured by expert clinician evaluation.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR architecture with the CLUE-V framework. First, we will generate baseline explanations using an attention-based feature attribution method adapted for transformer-based survival models. Second, we will implement an interactive counterfactual scenario engine using adversarial example techniques (e.g., FGSM) on the structured input embedding space to allow sensitivity probing. Third, prediction uncertainty will be quantified using Monte Carlo dropout ensembles. Crucially, a knowledge-grounded filter will cross-reference top explanatory features against curated ontologies (e.g., SNOMED CT) and clinical guidelines (e.g., AHA/ACC), flagging discrepancies for expert review before explanation finalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, open-source framework for generating validated explanations for time-to-event models, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating the clinical credibility (not just fidelity) of model explanations, and 3) empirical evidence on the types and frequency of conflicts between data-driven model rationales and established medical knowledge in large-scale EHR analyses.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond post-hoc interpretability by integrating real-time clinical knowledge validation as a core component of the explanation pipeline, and uniquely combines interactive counterfactuals, uncertainty presentation, and knowledge grounding specifically for the time-to-event prediction domain.

proposals.title: BITE: Co-designing Interpretability and Efficiency for Bedside Time-to-Event Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) for survival analysis, like MOTOR, offer powerful predictions but are computationally intensive and opaque, limiting their use in real-time, point-of-care settings. Clinicians need fast, private, and interpretable decision aids at the bedside to understand and act on dynamic patient risks, such as decompensation or readmission. Current model compression and post-hoc explanation techniques are applied sequentially, often degrading performance or producing unreliable explanations.
proposals.hypothesis: By jointly optimizing a time-to-event model for predictive accuracy, extreme parameter efficiency, and inherent architectural interpretability, we can create a single model capable of providing real-time, interactive risk predictions with faithful explanations on resource-constrained bedside hardware.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR architecture, specializing it for targeted clinical outcomes (e.g., ICU transfer). The core innovation is the integration of structured sparsity (via learned lottery tickets) and an interpretable latent space using a discrete autoencoder, trained end-to-end. Predictions will be generated via a sparse, additive hazard layer, where each feature's time-varying contribution is explicitly modeled. We will employ quantization-aware training and knowledge distillation from a full MOTOR model to achieve sub-100MB size suitable for mobile deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first ultra-efficient, inherently interpretable CFM for survival analysis deployable on edge devices, enabling low-latency, privacy-preserving bedside use. It will provide a novel co-design framework for compression and interpretability and deliver an interactive visualization tool that allows clinicians to manipulate input values and observe the consequent shift in the personalized risk curve.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its co-design of compression and intrinsic interpretability for survival CFMs, moving beyond post-hoc methods. It uniquely targets the creation of a unified, lightweight model architecture specifically for interactive, explanatory use at the point of care, a gap not addressed by large server-based models or generic compression techniques.

proposals.title: Explaining Time-to-Event Predictions in Clinical Practice: A Post-Hoc Interpretability Framework for the MOTOR Foundation Model
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for structured electronic health records (EHRs), like MOTOR, show promise for predicting time-to-event clinical outcomes. However, their adoption in clinical care is hindered by their 'black-box' nature. Clinicians require interpretable explanations of individual patient risk predictions to trust and act upon model outputs, especially for high-stakes decisions about treatment timing and patient management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, post-hoc interpretability framework, leveraging temporal attention mechanisms and counterfactual reasoning, can generate clinically plausible and actionable explanations for MOTOR's time-to-event predictions without degrading model performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we will adapt and extend post-hoc explanation techniques, such as SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) and integrated gradients, to the temporal, multi-modal architecture of MOTOR, explicitly handling sequences of clinical events. Second, we will generate dynamic, counterfactual explanations (e.g., 'If the patient's creatinine had remained below X, their predicted 1-year renal failure risk would decrease by Y%'). Model fidelity and explanation quality will be evaluated using established metrics (e.g., faithfulness, stability) and validated via structured clinician feedback on simulated cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated interpretability framework for time-to-event foundation models in EHRs, moving beyond static prediction explanations. It will deliver a practical tool to audit model reasoning and communicate personalized risk trajectories to clinicians, directly addressing a key barrier to the deployment of advanced AI in routine patient care and shared decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While interpretability for static models exists, explaining dynamic, longitudinal risk predictions from a pre-trained temporal foundation model like MOTOR is novel. Our approach uniquely combines temporal feature attribution with clinical counterfactuals tailored to the evolving nature of patient records.

proposals.title: Federated Pretraining of Time-to-Event Foundation Models with Robust Adapters and Global Knowledge Consolidation
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) is critical for training healthcare foundation models like MOTOR on distributed electronic health records while preserving privacy. However, extreme statistical heterogeneity across clinical sites—in patient demographics, treatment patterns, and data coding—causes standard FL to produce biased global models that underperform on underrepresented populations. This limits the equitable deployment of models for predicting time-to-event outcomes like treatment failure or adverse events.
proposals.hypothesis: Decoupling federated pretraining into a frozen global core and trainable site-specific adapters, coupled with a method to consolidate generally useful adapter-learned features, will yield a foundation model more robust to data heterogeneity and catastrophic forgetting than standard federated averaging.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR architecture by adding low-rank adaptation (LoRA) modules as site-specific adapters during federated pretraining, keeping the core transformer frozen. We will develop a novel server-side consolidation algorithm that periodically clusters adapter parameters across sites, identifies clusters representing generalizable feature shifts (vs. site-specific noise), and selectively merges these into the global core via a gradient-based integration step. Evaluation will use the 26-database network from prior adverse event studies to simulate a heterogeneous federation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical FL framework for pretraining healthcare foundation models that maintains global model integrity despite data skew. It will contribute a novel consolidation algorithm for continual knowledge integration in FL and demonstrate improved robustness in time-to-event prediction across diverse clinical databases compared to FedAvg and fine-tuning baselines.
proposals.novelty: While adapter-based personalization exists, the proactive analysis and fusion of beneficial adapter parameters into a frozen global core to combat catastrophic forgetting and bias in a medical FL setting is novel. This moves beyond static personalization towards a dynamically consolidating foundation model.

proposals.title: Generating Optimized Patient Pathways: A Diffusion and Inverse Optimization Framework for Counterfactual Intervention Design
proposals.motivation: Electronic health records (EHRs) offer a rich source of patient trajectories, but existing methods primarily identify risk or treatment effect heterogeneity. There is a critical need to move from passive identification to active design of personalized, preventive care sequences that delay adverse outcomes like heart failure or stroke. Current clinical decision support lacks a principled, data-driven method to generate and test novel, non-obvious sequences of care events that could serve as actionable intervention blueprints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model of patient timelines, coupled with an inverse optimization layer, can synthesize realistic, high-dimensional counterfactual sequences explicitly optimized to maximize time-to-event for a specified adverse outcome, and that these sequences encode novel, interpretable intervention strategies.
proposals.method: We will build on the MOTOR foundation model architecture for structured medical records to learn a latent representation of patient event sequences. A conditional diffusion model will be trained on this representation to generate realistic sequences. An inverse optimization module, formulated as a bilevel program, will guide the diffusion sampling process to identify latent vectors that, when decoded, produce sequences maximizing a reward function defined by time-to-adverse-event (e.g., using a Cox-based objective). The framework will be validated on a large-scale EHR database, such as those used in our prior multinational studies, focusing on a condition like atrial fibrillation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework for generative intervention design, producing synthetic yet realistic patient pathways that serve as 'optimized counterfactuals.' It will provide clinicians with a tool for hypothesis generation, suggesting specific timing and ordering of care events to delay adverse outcomes. The method advances the field from characterizing baseline rates and heterogeneity to actively engineering protective care sequences.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of a generative sequence model (diffusion) with an inverse optimization objective for time-to-event outcomes, creating a direct pathway from data to designed interventions. This goes beyond simulating from observed data or estimating effects, to generating and optimizing entirely new, plausible patient trajectories for clinical insight.

proposals.title: META-CARE: A Closed-Loop, Outcome-Steered Meta-Learning Framework for Clinically Viable Adaptation of Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Foundation models for electronic health records (EHRs) enable rapid adaptation to new clinical tasks via few-shot fine-tuning. However, current adaptation methods prioritize generic performance metrics (e.g., AUROC) without explicit consideration of real-world clinical utility, such as impact on patient outcomes, workflow integration cost, or diagnostic reliability. This gap risks deploying models that are technically accurate but clinically impractical or misaligned with healthcare priorities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a dynamic, clinically-grounded evaluation module into the meta-learning loop will steer the adaptation process to produce models that are not only accurate but also pre-emptively optimized for validated clinical utility and resource constraints in target tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend a time-to-event foundation model (e.g., MOTOR) with a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework. The inner-loop fine-tuning on a new clinical task (e.g., predicting bleeding risk on dabigatran) will be guided by an outer-loop meta-objective that incorporates real-time feedback from a Clinical Utility Evaluator. This evaluator will compute a composite reward based on simulated outcome impact (via a causal effect heterogeneity model), estimated workflow cost, and calibration metrics.
