The meta-learner's parameters will be updated to maximize this reward, creating a task-aware adaptation policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel closed-loop framework for outcome-oriented clinical model adaptation, 2) A principled method to quantify and optimize for clinical utility during few-shot learning, and 3) Empirical validation on heterogeneous treatment effect estimation and temporal phenotyping tasks using large-scale, multi-database EHR cohorts.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration of a real-time clinical utility signal directly into the meta-optimization process, moving beyond static loss functions to dynamically steer adaptation based on healthcare-specific value dimensions, which is absent in prior meta-learning or foundation model research.

proposals.title: EDGE-CITE: An Edge-Deployable Causal Inference Transformer for Real-Time Clinical Counterfactual Simulation
proposals.motivation: Clinical decision-making at the bedside requires timely, personalized risk assessment, but current predictive models often operate as black-box forecasts on centralized servers, creating latency and data privacy concerns. The ability to interactively simulate the causal effects of potential interventions on a patient's immediate trajectory is lacking, hindering personalized acute care. Edge computing offers a solution for low-latency, data-secure processing, but existing temporal foundation models like MOTOR are not designed for causal inference or resource-constrained deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, edge-optimized variant of a temporal event model can be adapted to perform valid, real-time estimation of individualized treatment effects by learning structured representations of clinical histories and enabling efficient counterfactual inference.
proposals.method: We will develop a compact transformer-based architecture, distilling knowledge from a pre-trained MOTOR-like model. The model will ingest a patient's structured electronic health record event sequence. To enable causal inference, we will integrate a targeted learning framework and g-computation, explicitly modeling intervention nodes within the temporal sequence. The model will be optimized for edge devices via techniques like quantization, pruning, and knowledge distillation, ensuring it can run on portable hardware and generate instant estimates of post-intervention risk trajectories for user-specified hypothetical actions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, validated method for real-time causal inference on edge devices, 2) an open-source, lightweight model for interactive clinical scenario planning that preserves data locality, and 3) an empirical evaluation of the trade-offs between model compactness and causal estimation fidelity in critical care settings.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely fuses the scalability of temporal foundation models with principled causal inference methods and edge-computing constraints, creating an interactive 'what-if' engine for clinicians, a capability not present in existing predictive or causal models pre-2026.

proposals.title: Circuit-Based Mechanistic Auditing of Multimodal Clinical Foundation Models for Robust Phenotype Discovery and Causal Hypothesis Generation
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on multimodal patient data promise to revolutionize risk prediction and decision support. However, their black-box nature and susceptibility to learning spurious correlations from electronic health records (EHR), genomics, and imaging raise critical concerns about robustness and trustworthiness for clinical deployment. Pre-2026, methods for validating that a model's internal reasoning aligns with biological mechanisms, rather than dataset artifacts, are nascent, creating a significant translational barrier.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying mechanistic interpretability techniques, specifically circuit analysis, to a multimodal CFM will reveal and validate internal sub-networks that implement robust, clinically coherent functions, and these circuits will serve as high-fidelity digital phenotypes for generating novel causal hypotheses.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR time-to-event foundation model architecture to integrate longitudinal EHR, genetic risk scores, and encoded imaging features. We will then audit the trained model using circuit-based interpretability methods (e.g., path patching, activation patching) to identify sparse computational subgraphs responsible for specific predictions, such as heart failure. We will evaluate circuit robustness by testing for shortcut learning (e.g., reliance on billing codes over lab trends) using counterfactual data and comparing circuit activations against established clinical pathways and known genetic associations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel auditing framework for CFMs, moving beyond feature attribution to algorithmic validation. It will yield a set of mechanistically interpretable, validated digital phenotypes for complex diseases. These circuits can inform the design of more robust models and serve as priors for targeted causal inference studies, like those in our prior dabigatran work.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely combines the scale of multimodal foundation models with the granularity of neuroscience-inspired circuit analysis for clinical audit, a fusion not yet explored in medical AI. It shifts the focus from *what* a model uses to *how* it computes, specifically for multimodal clinical timelines.

proposals.title: Learning Continuous-Time Protective Intervention Policies from Electronic Health Records using Neural ODEs and Adjoint Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: Clinical decision support often relies on static or discrete-time models, which fail to capture the continuous, irregularly sampled nature of patient physiology and treatment effects. This gap is critical for chronic conditions like atrial fibrillation, where optimal timing and intensity of interventions (e.g., anticoagulation) are key to preventing adverse outcomes like stroke. A method to discover personalized, time-varying intervention policies directly from observational EHR data could transform proactive care.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous-time model of patient health dynamics, learned via Neural ODEs from irregular EHR data, can be inverted via adjoint sensitivity methods to identify an optimal, personalized intervention policy that maximally delays a predicted time-to-event outcome.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR time-to-event foundation model architecture to learn a latent patient state governed by a Neural ODE, where the ODE's dynamics are conditioned on static features and time-varying treatments. Using the adjoint sensitivity method for efficient gradient computation through the ODE solver, we will formulate and solve an inverse optimal control problem: for a given patient trajectory, we will find the continuous-time treatment function that maximizes the predicted time-to-event (e.g., stroke), subject to realistic clinical constraints. Validation will use the multinational cohort infrastructure from prior work to assess policy performance against established treatments like dabigatran.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for discovering dynamic, personalized treatment strategies from observational data, moving beyond static effect estimation. It will contribute a new methodological paradigm for protective policy discovery in continuous time, directly applicable to EHR-based clinical decision support systems.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of Neural ODE-based patient trajectory modeling with adjoint-based inverse optimization for continuous-time policy discovery is novel; existing work on treatment effect heterogeneity or foundation models typically operates in discrete time and does not solve for optimal continuous control functions.

proposals.title: Counterfactual Causal Trajectories: Explaining Time-to-Event Predictions via Clinically Plausible Past Histories
proposals.motivation: Time-to-event models like MOTOR are increasingly used for clinical risk stratification, but their black-box nature limits trust and adoption. Existing explanation methods (e.g., SHAP) provide static feature attributions but fail to offer a temporally coherent, causal narrative linking a patient's past to a model's future prediction. Clinicians need to audit predictions by reasoning about the plausible historical trajectories that could lead to a forecasted outcome, a capability absent in current AI interpretability toolkits.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-model framework that infers the most probable, clinically plausible past trajectory leading to a given time-to-event prediction will produce more credible and actionable explanations than static feature importance methods, thereby increasing clinician trust and enabling more effective model error analysis.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-trained time-to-event foundation model (e.g., MOTOR) with a variational autoencoder (VAE) or a diffusion model conditioned on the target prediction and known patient anchor points. This 'retrospective' model will learn to generate sequences of past structured medical events (diagnoses, treatments, lab values) that are both consistent with the patient's known data and maximize the likelihood of the forward model's prediction. We will enforce clinical plausibility via regularization against real-world data patterns and incorporate causal constraints using established frameworks for longitudinal treatment effect estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for explainable AI in healthcare that provides causal, narrative-driven explanations for time-to-event predictions. It will advance the field of model interpretability beyond feature attribution by enabling counterfactual reasoning about patient history, directly supporting clinical sense-making and audit. The output will be a validated tool for improving trust in predictive models used for conditions like heart failure or atrial fibrillation.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely bridges forward-looking prediction with backward inference of plausible cause, generating entire counterfactual historical trajectories rather than point-in-time explanations. It moves beyond explaining *what* features matter to hypothesizing *how* a patient might have arrived at a predicted risk, a novel paradigm for model interrogation.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Domain Generalization and Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Diverse Clinical Prediction Tasks: A Multi-Database Study
proposals.motivation: The reliable deployment of clinical prediction models across diverse healthcare settings is hindered by distribution shifts in patient populations, coding practices, and clinical workflows. While recent foundation models like MOTOR and benchmarking studies for tasks like mortality have begun to address this, the performance of domain generalization (DG) and unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) methods remains poorly characterized for a broader spectrum of clinically actionable predictions. A systematic evaluation across a wider task range is needed to guide method selection and development for real-world clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative performance of state-of-the-art DG and UDA methods is highly task-dependent, and that their effectiveness on common benchmark tasks (e.g., mortality) does not generalize to other critical clinical predictions, such as hospital readmission or specific complication risks.
proposals.method: We will leverage the extensive, multi-national network of observational health databases (similar to those used in Shah's prior work) to create a benchmark suite. We will select 8-10 diverse prediction tasks, including readmission, acute kidney injury, heart failure exacerbation, and procedure recommendation. For each, we will train models on data from a subset of databases (sources) and evaluate on held-out databases (targets). We will benchmark methods including Invariant Risk Minimization (DG), Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks (UDA), and a fine-tuned MOTOR foundation model against standard supervised learning. Performance will be measured by time-dependent AUC and calibration metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive benchmark of DG/UDA methods across a diverse set of clinical tasks, providing empirical guidance for researchers and practitioners. It will identify task characteristics (e.g., prevalence, temporal dynamics) that influence method success and highlight gaps where current methods fail, directing future methodological research.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond the established, narrow set of clinical benchmarking tasks to systematically evaluate robustness methods on a wider array of predictions that are directly relevant for patient management and hospital operations, using a principled, multi-database framework established by the researcher's prior work.

proposals.title: Causal Pathways from NLP Benchmarks to Clinical Utility: A Framework for Validated Evaluation
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of clinical NLP models is benchmarked on technical tasks, but the link between these benchmark scores and tangible clinical utility—such as improved diagnostic decisions or workflow efficiency—remains poorly understood and correlational. This gap leads to inefficient allocation of research effort and potential misalignment between model capabilities and real-world clinical needs. A principled, causal understanding is needed to guide model development and evaluation toward meaningful patient and clinician outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a causal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) can be constructed to map how incremental improvements in specific, fine-grained NLP capabilities (e.g., temporal reasoning, negation detection) propagate to distinct dimensions of clinical utility, and that this propagation is non-uniform and task-dependent.
proposals.method: We will first construct a candidate causal DAG linking NLP sub-capabilities to utility dimensions (diagnostic accuracy, time-to-decision) via expert clinician elicitation. Using a foundation model like MOTOR as a base, we will generate model variants with controlled ablations or targeted fine-tuning to create differential performance on benchmark subtasks derived from datasets like MIMIC-III. We will then measure the causal effect of these capability differences on clinician performance in simulated diagnostic tasks and on retrospective treatment effect estimation (inspired by Shah's prior work on dabigatran) using targeted learning and g-computation for causal inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, causal framework that predicts which benchmark metrics are leading indicators of utility for specific clinical tasks. It will provide actionable guidance for the development and evaluation of clinical NLP models, moving the field from correlation to causation. The resulting methodology and open-sourced model variants will serve as a testbed for future utility-focused benchmarking.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond correlational analysis to experimentally establish causal links between NLP capabilities and clinical outcomes, integrating causal inference methods from epidemiology with controlled ablation studies from machine learning. It decomposes monolithic benchmarks into causal pathways, a novel approach for clinical NLP evaluation.

proposals.title: Validating Clinical NLP: A Prospective, Blinded Study Testing the Correlation Between Benchmark Metrics and Real-World Clinical Utility
proposals.motivation: The rapid integration of NLP models into clinical workflows (e.g., for note summarization, cohort identification) relies on benchmark leaderboards. However, the field lacks evidence that high scores on metrics like BLEU, ROUGE, or task-specific accuracy correlate with improved clinical decision-making or workflow efficiency. This mirrors the historical 'surrogate endpoint problem' in drug development, where biomarkers failed to predict true patient outcomes, leading to a regulatory framework for surrogate qualification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the correlation between standard NLP benchmark scores and blinded clinician assessments of real-world utility for specific clinical tasks is weak to non-existent, demonstrating that current metrics are unvalidated surrogates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, controlled study. For key tasks (e.g., radiology report summarization, medication list extraction), we will generate outputs from a spectrum of models (from rule-based to state-of-the-art transformers like fine-tuned ClinicalBERT, BioBERT, and GPT-3.5). Clinicians will be blinded to model identity and benchmark scores (e.g., BLEU, F1) and will rate outputs on utility scales (e.g., correctness, actionability, time-saving). We will use Spearman correlation and regression analyses to test the relationship between the surrogate metric scores and the clinical utility scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, prospective evidence quantifying the disconnect between NLP benchmarks and clinical utility. It will propose a validation framework analogous to the FDA's Biomarker Qualification Process, urging the community to develop and validate task-specific clinical utility metrics, moving beyond convenient proxies.
proposals.novelty: While model evaluation is well-studied, directly applying the formal surrogate endpoint validation paradigm from clinical trials to NLP evaluation is novel. The study's prospective, blinded design with practicing clinicians as the endpoint assessors moves beyond retrospective correlation studies common in the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Clinical Readiness Level (CRL) Framework: A Standardized, Staged Evaluation Roadmap for Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: The rapid emergence of Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) trained on electronic health records, such as MOTOR, creates an urgent need for standardized evaluation beyond traditional algorithmic metrics. Current validation is fragmented, lacking a clear, clinically-grounded pathway from proof-of-concept to safe, effective, and equitable deployment in patient care. This gap hinders developers, regulators, and healthcare systems in assessing true readiness and potential impact, risking premature deployment or stifling beneficial innovation.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the structured, staged Technology Readiness Level (TRL) framework from aerospace to healthcare will yield a valid and actionable Clinical Readiness Level (CRL) framework. This framework will provide a standardized, consensus-driven roadmap for evaluating the maturity and deployment readiness of CFMs, correlating with their real-world clinical utility and safety.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-stage, consensus-building study. First, we will map the canonical 9-level TRL structure to the clinical AI lifecycle via expert workshops with clinicians, data scientists, and regulators. Second, for each proposed CRL level, we will define specific validation tasks, datasets (e.g., from the Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI) network used in prior work), and success criteria, drawing from Shah's recent methods in time-to-event modeling and treatment effect heterogeneity evaluation. Third, we will empirically validate the framework's progression by applying it to a case-study CFM (e.g., a MOTOR variant), testing it through defined stages from in-silico benchmark performance (lower CRLs) to retrospective cohort simulation for treatment effect estimation (mid CRLs).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first formalized CRL framework, offering a practical tool for benchmarking CFM maturity. It will provide clear milestones for developers and a structured evidence requirement checklist for healthcare technology assessors and regulators, directly addressing the translational gap between AI research and clinical impact.
proposals.novelty: While model evaluation benchmarks exist, the explicit, structured translation of the TRL framework—a proven systems engineering tool for high-stakes environments—to clinical AI evaluation is novel. It moves beyond one-off performance metrics to a holistic, stage-gated systems view of readiness for patient care integration.

proposals.title: CLINICAL-FOUNDATIONS: A Framework for Evaluating Clinical Foundation Models on Real-World Healthcare Utility Metrics
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) trained on electronic health records, such as the time-to-event MOTOR model, has outpaced the creation of meaningful clinical evaluation standards. Current benchmarks focus on narrow predictive accuracy, failing to assess impact on downstream healthcare objectives like cost-efficiency, treatment pathway optimization, and long-term patient outcomes. This gap hinders the translation of CFMs from research artifacts into tools trusted for clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-dimensional evaluation framework, which integrates established clinical trial and health economics methodologies with CFM outputs, can robustly quantify a model's real-world clinical utility and reveal performance trade-offs invisible to standard ML metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct the framework by defining three core metric families: (1) **Clinical Outcome Surrogates**, adapting time-to-event and treatment effect heterogeneity methods from Shah's prior work to simulate patient trajectories under model-guided decisions; (2) **Resource Utilization**, using diagnosis-related group (DRG) and procedure cost weights to estimate downstream financial impact; and (3) **Safety & Robustness**, employing techniques from multinational cohort studies to establish baseline adverse event rates and measure model performance against them. The framework will be validated by applying it to a state-of-the-art CFM (e.g., a MOTOR-like architecture) on a large-scale EHR database (e.g., Optum or TriNetX), benchmarking its performance on simulated treatment decisions for conditions like atrial fibrillation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, clinically-grounded benchmark for CFMs, moving the field beyond accuracy-centric evaluation. It will deliver a standardized protocol for researchers and regulators to assess whether a model's predictions align with improving patient care and system efficiency, directly addressing the translational gap in medical AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing ML benchmarks, this framework explicitly bridges causal inference and health economics with foundation model evaluation, creating composite utility scores that reflect multi-stakeholder clinical priorities rather than isolated statistical performance.

proposals.title: SIM-CARE: A Simulation-Based Interactive Benchmark for Evaluating Sequential Diagnostic Reasoning in Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) rely on static question-answering, failing to capture the sequential, information-gathering nature of real-world clinical reasoning. This creates a significant gap in assessing a model's potential for safe and efficient clinical decision support, as it cannot evaluate diagnostic pathways, information-seeking efficiency, or the risk of premature closure. A dynamic, interactive benchmark is urgently needed to rigorously evaluate these capabilities before deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that benchmarking CFMs through interactive, sequential simulations will reveal significant variability in diagnostic reasoning quality—including efficiency, coherence, and safety—that is not captured by static accuracy metrics, and that these simulated pathways will correlate with expert assessments of clinical appropriateness.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment using time-to-event foundation models (e.g., extending the MOTOR architecture) trained on large-scale electronic health record databases (e.g., the 26-database network from prior work) to generate realistic, temporal patient trajectories. The benchmark will present CFMs with an initial clinical scenario, requiring them to interact with the simulator via a constrained API to request specific data (labs, history, imaging) over multiple turns. We will adapt and evaluate state-of-the-art CFMs (e.g., fine-tuned LLMs) on this platform, measuring metrics like diagnostic accuracy, number of steps to correct diagnosis, redundancy of queries, and adherence to safe diagnostic protocols derived from clinical guidelines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source, simulation-based benchmark for evaluating the interactive reasoning of CFMs, moving beyond static evaluation. It will produce a standardized framework for quantifying diagnostic pathway quality and safety, offering developers and clinical stakeholders a critical tool for model assessment and comparison prior to real-world implementation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on outcome prediction or static QA, this benchmark uniquely operationalizes the clinical reasoning *process* as an interactive, partially observable simulation, directly testing a model's ability to actively seek information and manage diagnostic uncertainty over time.

proposals.title: Causal Estimation of Clinical Foundation Model Impact on Hospital Operational Efficiency: A Multi-Center Difference-in-Differences Study
proposals.motivation: Health systems are rapidly piloting Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) like MOTOR for tasks such as early warning and treatment recommendation. However, pre-2026 evidence of their real-world operational impact is anecdotal or confounded by simultaneous quality initiatives. Hospital leaders lack causally robust, granular evidence on how CFMs affect specific, costly operational bottlenecks, hindering data-driven investment and deployment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: The staggered, heterogeneous deployment of a CFM for clinical decision support causes measurable, statistically significant reductions in specific, atomic operational KPIs (e.g., lab turnaround time, length-of-stay outliers) compared to matched control units, after rigorously accounting for temporal trends and confounding interventions.
proposals.method: We will employ a quasi-experimental, difference-in-differences (DiD) design with staggered adoption, leveraging electronic health record data from a consortium of health systems. Treatment units are hospital wards or services where a CFM (e.g., a MOTOR-derived model for sepsis prediction or discharge planning) is deployed. We will use propensity score matching on pre-period characteristics to construct control units. The primary analysis will use a two-way fixed effects estimator with unit and time fixed effects, applied to discrete KPIs extracted from the EHR audit trail. We will conduct robustness checks, including event-study plots and sensitivity analyses for parallel trends, as per recent high-impact studies on treatment effect heterogeneity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, causal framework for evaluating the operational ROI of CFMs, moving beyond predictive performance to attributable clinical impact. It will deliver a validated methodology and a set of causal effect estimates for specific KPIs, directly informing health system leadership on where and how to deploy AI for maximal operational gain.
proposals.novelty: While CFM evaluation focuses on predictive accuracy, and health services research uses DiD for policy changes, this study novelly applies modern causal inference for staggered adoption to isolate the effect of AI software itself on granular, atomic operational metrics, not composite scores.

proposals.title: The OPERA Framework: Operational Performance Evaluation of Real-world Applications for Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) like MOTOR has created a disconnect between academic evaluation and clinical utility. Current benchmarking relies on NLP metrics (e.g., F1-score, perplexity) that are poor proxies for tangible improvements in healthcare delivery. Health systems lack evidence-based tools to select models that genuinely enhance operational efficiency, patient throughput, or resource allocation, leading to potential misallocation of resources and failed deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a CFM's performance on standard NLP benchmarks will show zero or negative correlation with its ability to improve concrete, real-world operational Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in healthcare settings.
proposals.method: We will develop a benchmarking suite simulating real-world clinical workflows using large-scale, de-identified electronic health record databases (e.g., US claims, OMOP CDM). For tasks like cohort identification for clinical trials, automated clinical note summarization, and treatment effect heterogeneity screening, we will evaluate leading CFMs (e.g., MOTOR, GatorTron) against both NLP metrics and operational KPIs (e.g., time-to-cohort-identification, clinician review time saved, precision of high-risk patient identification). We will employ causal inference methods from prior work on treatment effect estimation to rigorously measure model-driven changes in operational outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated utility scorecard for CFMs, enabling health systems to make evidence-based deployment decisions. It will shift the evaluation paradigm from academic leaderboards to operational impact, providing a methodological framework for future real-world AI evaluation in healthcare.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond correlating model outputs with clinician judgments to directly measuring downstream operational efficiency, a critical but neglected dimension of evaluation. It formally tests the assumption that NLP benchmark performance predicts real-world clinical utility.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Operational Impact of Clinical Foundation Models: A Time-to-Event Analysis of Hospital Workflow Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Clinical foundation models (CFMs) trained on electronic health records promise to enhance clinical decision-making, but their utility for health system operations remains poorly defined and unmeasured. Pre-2026, healthcare systems face immense pressure to improve efficiency and resource allocation, yet there is a critical gap in evidence linking CFM-based predictions to tangible operational outcomes like length of stay, readmission timing, and bed turnover. This study aims to bridge that gap by providing a rigorous, quantitative framework to assess CFM operational utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a time-to-event foundation model, fine-tuned on structured hospital operational data, can generate predictions that, when integrated into workflow systems, significantly reduce key inefficiency metrics such as excess patient days and emergency department boarding times compared to standard operational heuristics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MOTOR (Time-to-Event Foundation Model) architecture, pre-trained on large-scale EHR data, and fine-tune it on granular, timestamped hospital operations data (e.g., bed requests, lab/imaging turnaround, consult orders) from a multi-hospital network. Using a difference-in-differences design with stepped-wedge implementation, we will measure the causal impact of model-generated predictions (e.g., for discharge probability, next-service need) on operational KPIs. Counterfactual evaluation will employ techniques from our prior work on treatment effect heterogeneity to identify which patient subgroups and hospital units derive the most operational benefit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, causal evidence framework for evaluating CFMs in an operational context, moving beyond diagnostic accuracy to measurable system efficiency. It will deliver a validated methodology and open-source toolkit for health systems to assess the return on investment for AI deployment, directly informing procurement and implementation decisions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on CFMs for clinical risk prediction, this study uniquely frames operational efficiency as a time-to-event problem and leverages a foundation model architecture specifically for this purpose, creating a direct causal link between model outputs and resource utilization outcomes that is absent in the current literature.

proposals.title: The CLINIC Framework: A Closed-Loop System for Measuring and Optimizing Clinician-Foundation Model Synergy in Electronic Health Record Workflows
proposals.motivation: Despite the proliferation of clinical foundation models (FMs), their integration into real-world workflows often treats them as passive recommendation engines, leading to automation bias, alert fatigue, and unclear impact on decision quality. There is a critical lack of frameworks that treat the clinician-FM dyad as a collaborative team, systematically measuring their interaction to improve both the model's utility and the clinician's performance. This gap hinders the safe and effective deployment of AI in high-stakes clinical settings where auditability and continuous improvement are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop, feedback-driven system that structures clinician-FM interaction as a mandatory, auditable dialogue will yield measurable improvements in the quality of clinical decisions (e.g., diagnostic accuracy, treatment appropriateness) compared to static FM recommendations, and the collected interaction data will enable the iterative refinement of both the FM's output and the workflow integration.
proposals.method: We will build upon the MOTOR foundation model architecture for structured EHR data. The system will integrate into a live EHR, presenting FM outputs (e.g., differential diagnoses, treatment suggestions) within a structured interface requiring clinician feedback (critique, confirmation, modification) via predefined actions before proceeding. We will employ causal inference methods, akin to those used in Shah's prior work on treatment effect heterogeneity, to analyze the longitudinal interaction data, clinician actions, and patient outcomes (e.g., 30-day readmission, time to correct diagnosis). A reinforcement learning component, using the clinician's feedback and downstream outcomes as a reward signal, will fine-tune the model's presentation strategy and suggestion timing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, deployable framework for clinician-in-the-loop AI that enforces structured engagement, 2) a new dataset of clinician-FM interaction trajectories linked to patient outcomes, and 3) empirical evidence on how specific interaction patterns correlate with decision quality. It will provide a principled methodology for moving from model integration to team performance optimization.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on model explainability or passive integration, this proposal explicitly designs the human-AI interaction as a closed-loop control system, using real-world workflow data and outcomes as the optimization signal. It shifts the evaluation focus from standalone model accuracy to the performance of the clinician-model team.

proposals.title: Integrating Clinical Foundation Model Predictions into EHR Workflows: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Actionable Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs), like the time-to-event MOTOR model, show promise in generating patient-specific predictions from electronic health records (EHRs). However, a critical gap exists in translating these model outputs into actionable, timely, and trustworthy information within the clinician's existing workflow. Without effective integration, these powerful tools risk being ignored or causing alert fatigue, limiting their real-world impact on patient care and treatment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a human-in-the-loop integration framework, which contextualizes CFM outputs within a clinician's immediate workflow and presents them through structured EHR interface modules, will significantly improve the perceived utility, interpretability, and actionability of model predictions compared to standalone model reports.
proposals.method: Building on the MOTOR architecture, we will develop an integration layer that processes its time-to-event predictions (e.g., for stroke or bleeding risk) into discrete, workflow-triggered alerts. We will employ causal inference methods, similar to those used in treatment effect heterogeneity studies, to identify high-impact prediction scenarios. The framework will be prototyped within a simulated EHR environment, using iterative design with clinician feedback to refine the presentation of risk scores, supporting evidence from the patient's timeline, and suggested next steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source framework for embedding CFM intelligence into clinical systems, moving beyond model performance to address practical adoption. It will yield concrete design principles for presenting predictive analytics in EHRs and generate empirical evidence on how such integration affects clinical decision-making processes and perceived workload.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on developing CFMs or evaluating their accuracy, this proposal directly addresses the translational challenge of workflow integration, combining Shah's expertise in large-scale clinical analytics, time-to-event modeling, and treatment effect evaluation with human-computer interaction principles for clinical settings.

proposals.title: Adversarial Red-Teaming for Inherently Robust Clinical Foundation Models: A Multi-Source Training Framework
proposals.motivation: Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) trained on multi-institutional electronic health records (EHRs) are prone to inheriting and amplifying dataset-specific biases, such as those related to patient demographics, coding practices, and site-specific care pathways. This compromises their safety and generalizability when deployed to new populations. Pre-2026, red-teaming has been used primarily for post-hoc evaluation of large language models, but its proactive integration into the medical AI training loop to preemptively address bias remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating adversarial red-teaming directly into the CFM training pipeline will produce models with measurably lower performance disparity across protected subgroups and greater robustness to distributional shifts, compared to models trained solely on standard empirical risk minimization.
proposals.method: We will extend a time-to-event foundation model architecture (e.g., MOTOR) as our base. During training, a dedicated adversarial red-team module will generate synthetic adversarial examples and bias probes targeting known (e.g., age, race) and latent subgroups. These will be created via controlled perturbations of input sequences and structured counterfactuals. A minimax objective will be employed, where the main model's loss is weighted to penalize failures on these adversarial batches, and synthetic adversarial examples will be augmented into the training data. Training will use federated learning principles on data from the Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics (OHDSI) network, similar to prior multi-database studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training paradigm for CFMs that bakes in robustness from first principles. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating bias in temporal medical models and a reusable open-source framework for adversarial training on structured EHR data. The resulting model will be a significant step towards safer, more equitable AI for clinical prediction.
proposals.novelty: This moves red-teaming from a passive, post-hoc audit tool to an active, integral component of the training objective for structured EHR-based models, creating a continuous adversarial feedback loop not previously applied in clinical foundation model development.

proposals.title: MECHANISTIC AUDIT: A Dynamic Causal Framework for Validating Clinical Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) trained on massive-scale electronic health records, like the MOTOR time-to-event model, are poised for deployment in high-stakes settings. Pre-2026, post-hoc interpretability tools (e.g., SHAP, attention) reveal correlations but cannot causally validate a model's internal reasoning, risking reliance on spurious patterns. A proactive, causal auditing framework is urgently needed to ensure model predictions are based on stable, clinically plausible mechanisms before integration into patient care pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying mechanistic interpretability techniques from language models (e.g., causal tracing, activation patching) to structured CFMs will reveal discrete, testable internal mechanisms whose causal role in predictions can be validated and whose stability across data distributions and model updates can be quantitatively monitored.
proposals.method: We will extend the MOTOR architecture to enable targeted interventions. The framework will: 1) Use gradient-based feature attribution and sparse autoencoders to identify candidate mechanistic subcircuits for key predictions (e.g., risk of hemorrhage on anticoagulants). 2) Systematically test causal hypotheses via activation patching on held-out patient sequences and on synthetically generated edge cases designed to expose confounding. 3) Implement a continuous monitoring loop that tracks mechanism consistency across temporal model updates (simulating distribution shifts) using statistical process control charts, generating a dynamic reliability report.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first operational framework for causal, mechanistic auditing of CFMs, moving beyond static performance metrics. It will deliver a formal methodology to distinguish robust clinical reasoning from brittle correlations, directly addressing concerns about treatment effect heterogeneity and adverse event prediction reliability. The output will be a validated software tool and reporting standard for model lifecycle governance.
proposals.novelty: While mechanistic interpretability is nascent in NLP, its systematic application as a dynamic auditing loop for structured medical CFMs is novel. The integration of causal hypothesis testing with continuous monitoring for mechanism drift represents a significant advance over static explainability or performance benchmarking common pre-2026.

ideas.title: A Closed-Loop, Feedback-Driven Framework for Measuring and Optimizing Clinician-Foundation Model Collaboration in Real Workflows
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a system that embeds foundation model outputs not as static recommendations, but as interactive partners in a structured clinical dialogue within the EHR. The system requires clinicians to actively engage with the model's reasoning—through critique, confirmation, or modification—before proceeding, creating an auditable decision trail. It continuously measures this interaction data, along with subsequent clinician actions and patient outcomes, to iteratively refine both the model's presentations and the workflow integration points. The goal is to move beyond integration to demonstrably improve the quality of the clinician-model team's decisions, using real-world feedback to close the loop on system design and model deployment.

ideas.title: Meta-Evaluated Clinical Adaptation: A Closed-Loop Framework for Task-Aware, Outcome-Oriented Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel closed-loop framework that integrates meta-learning for fast adaptation with a dynamic, clinically-grounded evaluation system. The core innovation is that the meta-learner's adaptation process is continuously evaluated and steered by real-time feedback from healthcare-specific metrics—such as predicted impact on patient outcomes, clinical workflow cost, and diagnostic reliability—during the few-shot fine-tuning phase. This creates a task-aware adaptation mechanism where the model learns not just to adapt quickly to a new clinical task, but to adapt in a way that is pre-emptively aligned with validated clinical utility and resource constraints. Consequently, the framework ensures that rapidly customized models are both performant and clinically viable from their initial deployment, bridging the gap between technical adaptation speed and practical healthcare value.

ideas.title: Decomposing the NLP Benchmark-to-Clinical-Utility Pathway: A Causal Mapping Study
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple correlation test to construct a causal model linking specific NLP capabilities, as measured by tailored benchmark subtasks, to distinct dimensions of clinical utility. Through controlled experiments with clinicians in simulated and retrospective real-world settings, the study measures how incremental improvements in benchmark performance propagate—or fail to propagate—to changes in diagnostic accuracy, workflow efficiency, and decision confidence. The outcome is a validated framework that predicts which benchmark metrics are predictive of utility for given clinical tasks, providing actionable guidance for model development and evaluation.

ideas.title: Bedside Interpretable Time-to-Event (BITE) Models
ideas.description: This research develops ultra-efficient, compressed clinical foundation models (CFMs) specialized for time-to-event predictions that can run locally on bedside mobile devices or hospital hardware. The core innovation is the co-design of the model compression techniques with inherently interpretable architectures, such as sparse additive models or discrete latent variable models, tailored for survival analysis. This enables the model to generate immediate, interactive visual explanations (e.g., showing how changing a lab value shifts the predicted risk curve) directly on the clinician's device. The result is a private, low-latency decision aid that not only provides a prediction but also fosters clinical understanding and trust through embedded, real-time interpretability at the point of care.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Clinical Foundation Models via Operational Utility, Not NLP Scores
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel benchmarking paradigm for Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) that rejects standard NLP metrics as proxies for clinical value. Instead, it develops a suite of task-specific evaluations that directly measure a CFM's impact on concrete operational efficiency metrics, such as patient throughput, staff time saved, or resource allocation accuracy. The core hypothesis is that models achieving high scores on traditional benchmarks will show zero or negative correlation with improvements in these real-world operational KPIs. The outcome will be a validated utility scorecard for CFMs that enables health systems to select and deploy models based on proven operational impact rather than academic benchmark leaderboards.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Clinical Foundation Models via Interactive, Sequential Reasoning Simulations
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic benchmarking platform that simulates realistic, multi-turn clinical encounters to evaluate the reasoning capabilities of Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs). Instead of static Q&A, models interact with a simulated patient or clinical database, must actively request relevant information (e.g., lab tests, history details), and provide evolving assessments and differential diagnoses. The benchmark measures not just final accuracy but the efficiency, coherence, and safety of the diagnostic pathway, providing a far more robust assessment of a model's potential for real-world clinical decision support.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Technology Readiness Level' Framework for Clinical Foundation Model Evaluation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's Technology Readiness Level (TRL) framework, this research proposes to develop and validate a 'Clinical Readiness Level' (CRL) framework for evaluating Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs). The TRL model's success lies in its staged, criteria-based assessment of technological maturity for high-stakes environments. Adapted to healthcare, the CRL framework would define a 9-level progression from basic research to full clinical integration. Each level would specify appropriate healthcare-specific validation metrics, such as algorithmic fairness and robustness at lower levels, progressing to simulated diagnostic accuracy and workflow efficiency, and culminating in real-world measurements of cost-effectiveness, clinician burden reduction, and direct patient health outcomes at the highest levels. This framework aims to provide a standardized, clinically-grounded roadmap for developers, evaluators, and healthcare regulators to assess the true readiness and potential impact of CFMs.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Causal Auditing Framework for Clinical Foundation Models Using Mechanistic Interpretability
ideas.description: This research creates a system that uses mechanistic interpretability not as a one-off analysis tool, but as the core of a dynamic, causal auditing loop for Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs). The framework actively formulates testable hypotheses about the causal mechanisms behind model predictions and rigorously tests them through controlled interventions on model components, input data, and synthetic edge cases. It evaluates the stability and robustness of these mechanisms across model updates and potential data distribution shifts, distinguishing genuine clinical reasoning from spurious correlations. The output is a continuously updated 'reliability report' for critical model predictions, enabling proactive detection of failure modes and ensuring audit trails that remain valid over the model's lifecycle.

ideas.title: Robust, Calibrated Multi-Event Learning with Safe Fallback
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework for modeling competing and correlated time-to-event outcomes that is explicitly designed to be safe against model misspecification. It combines flexible, nonparametric estimation of dependency structures with continuous monitoring of calibration and domain shift. A key component is a gating mechanism that selectively employs the full joint model or reverts to independent marginal models for different patient or data subgroups, based on real-time performance diagnostics. The final system guarantees predictive performance that is at least as good as using separate models, while capturing multivariate dependencies when the data provides sufficient, reliable evidence.

ideas.title: Counterfactual Causal Trajectories: Explaining Time-to-Event Predictions via Clinically Plausible Past Histories
ideas.description: This research develops a framework where a time-to-event model's prediction (e.g., 'high risk of heart failure in 3 years') is not just explained with feature importance, but is actively grounded by a dual model that infers the most probable preceding clinical trajectory leading to that prediction. The system generates a counterfactual patient history—a sequence of plausible past events, measurements, and interventions—that is both consistent with the patient's known data and optimally leads to the model's forecasted outcome. This allows clinicians to interrogate the prediction by evaluating the credibility of the inferred past, providing a powerful, causal narrative for model trust and error analysis that neither forward-looking explanations nor retrospective inference alone can offer.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Clinical Foundation Model Impact on Discrete Hospital Operational KPIs
ideas.description: This research establishes a causal inference framework to isolate and quantify the true effect of Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) on specific, atomic hospital operational metrics. Instead of a composite 'utility' score, the study measures impact on discrete, pre-registered Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)—such as order-to-result time, length-of-stay variance, or bed-turnover delay—using a quasi-experimental, difference-in-differences design across matched clinical units. The methodology leverages natural variation in deployment timing and depth to create robust counterfactuals, controlling for confounding factors like parallel process changes. The result is a set of causally attributable, actionable effect sizes that inform precise ROI calculations and deployment strategies for health system leaders.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Auditing of Multimodal Clinical Foundation Models for Robustness and Causal Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction trains a massive clinical foundation model (CFM) on integrated EHR, genomics, and imaging timelines, then applies circuit-based mechanistic interpretability as an internal audit tool. The goal is to reverse-engineer the model's internal algorithms to find sub-networks that implement specific, clinically relevant functions—like predicting heart failure from a combination of genetic markers, longitudinal lab values, and imaging features. This audit process validates whether the model's predictions arise from robust, biologically plausible multimodal integration or from shortcut learning and dataset biases. The resulting validated circuits can then serve as high-fidelity, interpretable digital phenotypes for causal hypothesis generation and more trustworthy clinical decision support.

ideas.title: Clinically Grounded, Uncertainty-Aware Explanation and Validation for Time-to-Event Predictions
ideas.description: This research develops an explanation system that moves beyond post-hoc interpretability to integrate direct clinical validation. It generates explanations for MOTOR's predictions that are coupled with interactive counterfactual scenarios, allowing clinicians to probe model reasoning by altering patient variables. The system explicitly quantifies and presents prediction uncertainty and identifies the input variables with the highest sensitivity. Crucially, it incorporates a knowledge-grounded filter that cross-references the model's primary explanatory features against curated medical ontologies and guidelines, flagging discrepancies for expert review before the explanation is finalized, thereby ensuring explanations are both faithful to the model and clinically credible.

ideas.title: Edge-Enabled Causal Intervention Engine for Clinical Trajectories
ideas.description: This research develops a lightweight, edge-deployable variant of a temporal model (like MOTOR) that is specifically architected for causal inference. The compact model runs directly on clinical edge devices (e.g., bedside monitors, portable diagnostics) to process a patient's ongoing event sequence. Its core function is to invert the standard predictive direction: instead of just forecasting a future risk, it allows clinicians to input hypothetical interventions (e.g., 'administer drug X now,' 'increase monitoring frequency') and instantly estimates how each intervention would alter the patient's subsequent risk profile. This enables real-time, interactive scenario planning at the bedside, supporting personalized intervention decisions while keeping sensitive data local and secure.

ideas.title: Generative Counterfactual Intervention Design via Diffusion-Guided Inverse Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction combines a diffusion model for generating realistic patient event sequences with an inverse optimization framework that identifies sequences maximizing time to an adverse outcome. The synergy enables the generation of synthetic 'counterfactual' patient timelines—realistic sequences that are explicitly optimized to be protective. These generated sequences can then be analyzed to distill novel, non-obvious intervention strategies, such as specific timing or ordering of care events, that delay the target outcome. This provides a powerful tool for hypothesis generation in clinical decision support and preventive care planning, moving beyond identification to the simulation of optimized patient pathways.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Surrogate Endpoint Problem' from Drug Development to Challenge NLP Metric Validity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high scores on standard NLP benchmarks indicate clinical usefulness by drawing a direct analogy to the 'surrogate endpoint problem' in clinical pharmacology. In drug development, surrogate biomarkers (like tumor shrinkage) were long used as proxies for true patient outcomes until rigorous studies proved they could be dangerously uncorrelated. The analogous solution was to mandate formal validation pathways to qualify a surrogate. Adapted to NLP, we propose treating metrics like BLEU or benchmark accuracy as unvalidated surrogates for the clinical endpoint of utility. The research would involve conducting prospective, controlled studies where clinicians assess the output of various NLP models on real clinical tasks, blinded to the model's benchmark performance. The primary analysis would test for correlation between the surrogate metric scores and the real-world utility scores, with the goal of providing definitive evidence to spur the development of a new framework for validating NLP metrics intended for clinical use, moving the field beyond convenient but potentially meaningless proxies.

ideas.title: Red-Teaming for Bias-Aware, Multi-Source CFM Training
ideas.description: This research proposes to integrate adversarial red-teaming directly into the training pipeline of Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs) built on diverse, multi-institutional EMR data. Instead of using red-teaming only for post-hoc evaluation, its techniques will generate adversarial examples and bias probes that actively inform the data sampling, loss weighting, and synthetic data augmentation during training. This creates a model that learns not just from the statistical patterns in the heterogeneous data, but also from simulated safety failures, leading to inherent robustness. The outcome is a CFM whose generalizability is consciously engineered to withstand targeted attacks on its biases, making it safer and more reliable for deployment across unseen healthcare settings.

ideas.title: Federated Pretraining of MOTOR with Heterogeneity-Robust Adapters and Global Knowledge Consolidation
ideas.description: This research designs a federated learning system for pretraining foundation models in healthcare that is robust to extreme data heterogeneity across sites. The architecture decouples learning into a frozen global model core and trainable, site-specific adapter modules, preventing local data skew from corrupting shared representations. A novel consolidation algorithm periodically analyzes adapter parameters to identify and fuse generally useful features into the global core, mitigating catastrophic forgetting. This approach ensures the final model performs robustly across all patient populations and clinical specialties represented in the federation.

ideas.title: Continuous-Time Protective Policy Discovery via Neural ODEs and Adjoint Sensitivity
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the inverse problem of finding protective sequences with Neural ODE modeling of patient trajectories. The core idea is to use Neural ODEs to learn a continuous-time model of a patient's latent health state dynamics from irregularly sampled Electronic Health Record (EHR) data. The novel synergy is to then apply adjoint sensitivity methods to this trained model to solve the inverse problem: not for discrete event patterns, but for an optimal *continuous-time policy* (e.g., a function of time specifying medication intensity or lifestyle adjustment) that maximally delays the predicted adverse outcome. This allows for the discovery of personalized, dynamically adjustable intervention strategies that work within the continuous physiological processes between discrete clinical events.

ideas.title: Continuous-Time, Edge-Optimized Clinical State Estimators
ideas.description: This research develops ultra-efficient clinical foundation models that use neural ordinary differential equations (Neural ODEs) as their core dynamical engine, specifically designed to run locally on mobile or embedded hospital hardware. The model learns a compact, continuous-time representation of patient physiology that evolves between the discrete, irregular observations recorded in the EHR. By fusing compression techniques for the model weights with the inherent parameter efficiency and continuous interpolation of Neural ODEs, the system can provide real-time, low-latency estimates of a patient's latent state and project short-term trajectories without cloud dependency. This enables novel applications like adaptive monitoring, early warning for deterioration between measurements, and personalized treatment simulation at the point of care, all while maintaining strong privacy and operating under hardware constraints.

ideas.title: Adversarial Red-Teaming for Longitudinal Foundation Models Across Domains
ideas.description: This research fuses the transfer of medical longitudinal pretraining (MOTOR) to non-medical domains with AI safety red-teaming techniques. It develops a systematic adversarial testing protocol specifically designed for foundation models trained on sequential event data from diverse domains like finance, education, or logistics. The core innovation is using red-teaming to probe for novel, temporally-contingent failures—such as compounding bias in credit decisions or exploitable patterns in student performance predictions—that only emerge in longitudinal reasoning. This establishes a domain-agnostic benchmark for the robustness and safety of sequential foundation models before real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Federated Learning for Clinical Models via a Physics-Informed Digital Twin Framework
ideas.description: This idea inverts the need for massive, centralized EMR datasets by drawing an analogy from climate modeling. In climate science, high-fidelity global models are built on physics simulators, not a monolithic observational dataset; sparse, real-world data from distributed sources is used for validation and refinement. We adapt this solution by proposing the development of clinical foundation models anchored in a 'physics-informed' digital twin framework based on established biomedical knowledge. The core model is pre-trained on synthetic data generated from physiological simulations. During federated learning cycles, participating institutions use their local, private EMR data not as the primary training material, but as a tool to perform localized parameter estimation, bias correction, and personalization of this universal model. This approach shifts the paradigm from learning patterns purely from data to calibrating a first-principles model with federated data, thereby enhancing privacy, improving model generalizability, and reducing the dependency on centralized data aggregation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Knowledge-Infused Clinical Edge Models (DKICEM)
ideas.description: This approach develops ultra-efficient clinical foundation models for bedside devices that integrate a static, compressed reasoning core with a dynamically updatable knowledge graph. A secure, minimal-footprint synchronization client on the device regularly fetches and applies discrete, verified updates—such as new drug contraindications or treatment guidelines—from a curated, version-controlled medical knowledge repository. This ensures the local model's clinical recommendations remain current and safe without requiring frequent, bandwidth-intensive full model retraining or redeployment, maintaining offline functionality while mitigating the risk of knowledge obsolescence.

ideas.title: Heterogeneity-Aware Federated Clinical Foundation Models with Stabilized Synthetic Curriculum
ideas.description: This research develops a clinical foundation model training system designed to overcome the fundamental heterogeneity and data scarcity challenges of pure federated or synthetic approaches. It combines a federated learning backbone with a curriculum that strategically injects high-fidelity synthetic data, generated per institution to fill local distribution gaps, to prevent model divergence and forgetting. A central consensus model, trained on aggregated, privacy-secure embeddings, provides a stabilizing signal to align learning across sites and assess generalization. The result is a robust model that leverages decentralized real data while actively compensating for its inherent biases and sparsity, ensuring clinical reliability without centralized data collection.

ideas.title: Incorporate richer patient preference heterogeneity

ideas.title: Adapting Geostatistical Kriging for Diffusion-Based Event Sequence Imputation
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from geostatistics, where Kriging interpolates spatial data based on correlation structures. The analogous solution uses observed sample points and a variogram model to predict values at unobserved locations with quantified uncertainty. Adapted to the medical event sequence context, we propose using diffusion models to learn the complex 'temporal variogram'—the conditional probabilities and dependencies between clinical events over time from real, sparse patient records. This learned model can then perform probabilistic interpolation and generation of coherent event sequences between known anchor points (e.g., initial diagnosis and outcome), creating synthetic, realistic patient timelines that respect the underlying temporal correlations. This enables high-quality data augmentation for rare pathways and privacy-preserving synthetic data generation, as the output is a statistically faithful but novel patient history.

ideas.title: Adapting Causal Pathway Diagrams from Ecology to Explain Time-to-Event Predictions
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecosystem modeling, where causal pathway diagrams (like Structural Equation Models) are used to explain complex, multivariate drivers of a future event (e.g., population collapse). The analogous problem is explaining a black-box model's time-to-event prediction. The adapted idea is to develop a novel interpretability method for MOTOR that generates patient-specific causal pathway diagrams. These visual explanations would map the key clinical features and their inferred directional influences that collectively lead to the model's predicted time-to-event, such as heart failure admission. This provides clinicians with an intuitive, audit-able 'narrative' of the prediction, grounding the model's output in a familiar framework of clinical causality and progression.

ideas.title: A Modular Ensemble of Specialized Clinical Models via a Mixture-of-Experts Architecture
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that clinical foundation models (CFMs) must be general-purpose by proposing a modular architecture inspired by the brain's functional organization. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, where specialized brain regions are selectively engaged for specific tasks, the solution is to create a collection of small, specialized models for distinct clinical tasks (e.g., imaging, genomics, notes). These models are integrated via a mixture-of-experts-style gating mechanism that dynamically routes inputs, allowing the system to behave as a tailored, composite model for each case. This approach aims to outperform a single large model by improving efficiency, interpretability (through clearer module responsibility), and performance on specialized clinical sub-tasks without catastrophic forgetting.

ideas.title: Adapting Air Traffic Control Alert Integration for CFM Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to study the effective integration of Clinical Foundation Model (CFM) outputs into clinical decision support workflows and EHR interfaces by drawing an analogy from aviation human-computer interaction, specifically air traffic control (ATC) systems. The analogous problem is the integration of automated collision detection and resolution advisories (e.g., TCAS) into the high-stakes, fast-paced ATC workflow. That field solved it by developing comprehensive 'alerting philosophies' featuring silent background monitoring, non-intrusive status indicators, and a tiered hierarchy of visual and auditory alerts matched to severity, all governed by strict design protocols for information salience, acknowledgment, and dismissal to prevent alarm fatigue and support the human operator's ultimate authority. Adapted to the clinical context, this research would investigate how to design and validate a similar tiered integration framework for CFMs. It would explore how to map CFM outputs (e.g., probability scores, diagnostic suggestions, critical risk flags) to appropriate presentation modalities within the EHR—from subtle UI color cues and sidebar summaries to modal alerts—based on clinical urgency and context. The core study would focus on developing and testing interface protocols that embed CFM support seamlessly, aiming to augment clinician judgment and workflow efficiency without causing disruption or over-reliance, directly analogous to the successful human-factors engineering applied in aviation safety systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Tomography from Internet Engineering to Audit Clinical Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in Internet Engineering—network tomography—to the challenge of auditing Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs). In network tomography, the internal state of a complex, opaque network (like the internet) is inferred by sending controlled probe packets from external points and statistically analyzing the end-to-end outcomes to deduce internal link properties. Analogously, for a CFM, we can design a suite of targeted input 'probes' (e.g., selectively removing or altering clinical concepts in a note, introducing synthetic but plausible confounding factors) and then trace the propagation of these perturbations through the model's layers. By analyzing the correlation between specific input alterations, the resulting activation patterns in intermediate neurons or attention heads, and shifts in the final prediction, we can algorithmically infer critical computational sub-circuits (the 'clinical decision pathways') for tasks like diagnosis or risk stratification. This provides a scalable, hypothesis-driven audit framework to understand how specific predictions are made, identifying potential reliance on spurious correlations or medically unsound reasoning.

ideas.title: Transfer Learning from Synthetic Populations for Rare Disease TTE Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that effective Time-to-Event (TTE) models require massive, real-world pretraining datasets. Drawing an analogy from Ecology, where models for rare species are bootstrapped using synthetic data derived from the known niches of related common species, we propose a method for rare diseases. The analogous solution of 'ecological niche modeling' is adapted by first pre-training a TTE model on a large, synthetically generated cohort. This synthetic data is engineered to reflect the understood pathophysiology, risk factors, and progression dynamics of the target rare disease, informed by knowledge from related more prevalent conditions. The model, thus 'primed' with relevant structural and temporal patterns, is then fine-tuned on the limited real patient data available for the rare condition. This approach relaxes reliance on large-scale real pretraining data by leveraging mechanistic knowledge to create a functionally informative synthetic pre-training distribution.

ideas.title: MOTOR-Lite: A Confidence-Aware, Hybrid Edge-Cloud Model for Robust Clinical Deployment
ideas.description: This project develops a hybrid clinical inference system centered on a compact MOTOR variant designed for edge devices. The core innovation is a training and deployment framework that combines multi-fidelity knowledge distillation with a confidence-based gating mechanism. The lightweight model runs efficiently on-device but is trained to recognize its own limitations, automatically deferring high-stakes or uncertain cases to a secure cloud-based instance of the full MOTOR model. This approach guarantees robust performance, maintains usability in low-bandwidth settings, and provides a formal safety boundary for clinical deployment.

ideas.title: Orchestrated Clinical Model Ensemble with Context-Aware Fusion
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid architecture where a suite of small, specialized clinical models is governed by a dedicated orchestrator. The orchestrator is designed to manage the complexities of real-world clinical data, integrating disparate modalities (text, imaging, time-series) and maintaining longitudinal patient context. It dynamically routes queries, resolves potential conflicts between specialist outputs, and synthesizes them into a unified, actionable clinical summary. The core hypothesis is that this orchestrated system will surpass both a monolithic foundation model and an uncoordinated ensemble in clinical coherence, accuracy, and practical utility.

ideas.title: Apply Few-Shot Meta-Learning with a Physics-Informed Prior for Clinical Task Adaptation
ideas.description: The original idea aims to use meta-learning for fast adaptation to new clinical tasks with few examples. Drawing an analogy from computational physics, where physics-informed neural networks use known physical laws as a prior to solve new problems with minimal data, we adapt this solution. The expanded research idea is to apply few-shot meta-learning with a clinical knowledge prior for task adaptation. This involves training a meta-learner on diverse clinical tasks while incorporating a structured prior that encodes known biomedical invariances, physiological constraints, or causal relationships. This prior, acting as a regularizer or architectural module, ensures the model's rapid adaptations are clinically plausible, leading to faster convergence and more reliable performance when fine-tuning with only a handful of labeled examples for a novel diagnostic or prognostic task.

ideas.title: Dual: Bayesian Retrodiction of Clinical Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from historical geology and paleontology, where scientists retrodict past environments from present-day evidence, to the clinical domain. The analogous problem is reconstructing a sequence of past events from a known final state, solved by applying established laws to the observed outcome within a probabilistic framework. The adapted idea is to create a Bayesian retrodiction engine for clinical histories. Given a known future diagnosis or event (e.g., a pancreatic cancer diagnosis), the system would use formalized models of disease progression and population data to infer and rank the most probable sequences of preceding symptoms, test results, and exposures that a patient likely experienced, effectively scaling the 'dual' prediction of past from future.

ideas.title: Apply astronomical light curve contrastive learning to EHR event sequences
ideas.description: This idea adapts a contrastive learning solution from time-domain astrophysics to the problem of learning representations from Electronic Health Records (EHR). In astronomy, contrastive pretraining on irregularly sampled, noisy stellar light curves has proven effective for tasks like variable star classification. The analogous solution involves creating positive pairs from different augmented views of the same star's light curve and negative pairs from different stars. Adapted to EHR, we propose a patient-level contrastive pretraining objective where multiple, possibly augmented, temporal segments of a patient's event sequence (diagnoses, medications, lab results) are pulled together in the embedding space, while segments from different patients are pushed apart. This method aims to learn robust, patient-centric sequence representations that are resilient to irregular clinical sampling and focus on underlying health states, improving downstream tasks like disease progression modeling and patient stratification.

ideas.title: Applying Paleontological Data Curation Frameworks to EMR Data Quality
ideas.description: This idea transfers data curation methodologies from paleontology to improve EMR data quality for Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs). In paleontology, researchers build high-quality evolutionary datasets from fragmented, heterogeneous fossil records using 'chronostratigraphic frameworks.' These frameworks establish a standardized temporal hierarchy and process for cross-referencing specimens, assigning confidence scores to dating accuracy to clean and weight data. Adapted to the clinical domain, we propose developing a 'Clinical Event Stratigraphy'—a formal, hierarchical timeline of clinical events. By applying data-centric AI techniques, this framework would be used to algorithmically align messy, real-world EMR entries, validate temporal sequences, and assign data-quality confidence scores. This process would transform raw, often inconsistent EMR data into curated, temporally coherent, and high-fidelity training sets, directly enhancing the reliability and performance of CFMs trained on this structured temporal data.

ideas.title: Adapt Control Theory's State Observer Design for Continuous EHR Trajectory Estimation
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from Control Theory, where state observers estimate the full internal state of a dynamical system from sparse and noisy output measurements. The analogous solution involves using a model of the system dynamics (ODEs) and a correction mechanism based on measurement discrepancies. Adapted to clinical research, we propose modeling patient trajectories with a neural ODE to represent continuous latent health dynamics. When sparse, irregular EHR events (like lab results or vital signs) occur, an observation update step—inspired by observer design—is applied. This step fuses the neural ODE's prior prediction with the new clinical evidence, generating a corrected and continuously evolving estimate of the patient's state. The result is a framework that provides a smooth, uncertainty-quantified trajectory of patient health between the discrete points recorded in the EHR, enabling better prediction of future events and understanding of disease progression.

It employs classifiers to assess evidence quality and agreement, explicitly identifying knowledge gaps and contradictions. The CFM is then constrained to generate outputs that include a structured confidence score, a traceable rationale linking to source documents, and clear caveats or deferral statements when evidence is insufficient or conflicting, ensuring clinical safety over mere relevance.
