ideas.title: Edge-optimized CFMs via model distillation and specialized kernels
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational fluid dynamics, where complex simulations are made to run on embedded systems via reduced-order, hardware-aware surrogate models. The analogous solution involves distilling a high-fidelity 'teacher' model into a compact 'student' model optimized for a specific embedded architecture. Adapted to clinical foundation models (CFMs), the research would develop a process to train a large cloud-based CFM, then use progressive knowledge distillation and neural architecture search to create a highly compressed student CFM. This student model would be co-designed with specialized neural kernels for target mobile or hospital hardware (e.g., specific DSPs or NPUs), ensuring ultra-efficient local execution for bedside diagnostics and monitoring without compromising critical clinical accuracy.

ideas.title: A Multi-Modal Pre-training Strategy for Clinical Events Using Cross-Modal Alignment
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending the MOTOR foundation model by integrating unstructured clinical text into its pre-training, analogous to how remote sensing models fuse multi-modal satellite data. Drawing from solutions in computational remote sensing where models like Prithvi use cross-modal alignment (e.g., contrastive learning between images and sensor reports), we adapt this approach to the clinical domain. The adapted idea is to pre-train MOTOR on paired structured EHR events and unstructured clinical notes using a cross-modal masked reconstruction objective. This will force the model to align information across modalities, learning to enrich structured event representations with the nuanced context from clinical text, ultimately creating a more robust foundation for understanding complex patient trajectories.

ideas.title: Apply Penetration Testing from Cybersecurity to CFM Evaluation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the formal, methodological approach of penetration testing from cybersecurity to create a structured adversarial evaluation framework for Clinical Foundation Models (CFMs). In cybersecurity, pen-testing involves authorized, systematic attacks to find vulnerabilities before malicious actors do. The analogous solution is adapted by having multidisciplinary teams (clinicians, ethicists, AI safety researchers) act as ethical 'clinical pen-testers.' They would employ a phased methodology—similar to reconnaissance, scanning, and exploitation—to systematically probe CFMs for clinical safety failures, harmful biases, and diagnostic blind spots, especially under rare or adversarial input conditions, thereby generating a rigorous 'safety audit' report for the model.

ideas.title: MOTOR-Lite: A Knowledge-Distilled Model for Edge Clinical Deployment
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from astrophysics and spacecraft systems, where extreme resource constraints on platforms like CubeSats necessitate 'onboard autonomy' via lossy compression and specialized lightweight algorithms. The analogous solution involves creating purpose-built, highly optimized code that performs essential feature extraction and decision-making locally, rather than transmitting raw data. Adapted to the clinical edge context, we propose MOTOR-Lite: a compact model developed through knowledge distillation and structured pruning. A full-scale MOTOR teacher model trains a student model to replicate only the critical diagnostic features for a specific clinical task. The student architecture is then aggressively scaled down and hardware-optimized, removing non-essential components to create a variant deployable on resource-constrained edge devices in clinical settings, enabling local, real-time analysis without continuous cloud dependency.

ideas.title: Jump-Aware Stochastic Differential Equations for Abrupt and Continuous Clinical Dynamics
ideas.description: This model represents patient health trajectories using a Jump Stochastic Differential Equation (JSDE) framework that explicitly decouples smooth physiological drift, stochastic diffusion (random fluctuations), and discrete jump processes. A neural marked temporal point process governs the timing and characteristics of jumps, which correspond to clinical interventions, acute events, or measurement shocks. This hybrid approach provides a more faithful representation of real EHR data, enabling accurate forecasting of both gradual decline and sudden deterioration, as well as counterfactual reasoning about the impact of hypothetical interventions.

ideas.title: Causal Intervention via Counterfactual Sequence Engineering
ideas.description: This idea inverts the causal direction for intervention by drawing an analogy from structural engineering. In that field, finite element analysis models are used not merely to predict failure but to actively design interventions—altering material properties and shapes in the simulation to find configurations that prevent failure. Adapting this solution, we propose using sequential risk prediction models not just for forecasting, but to engineer counterfactual event sequences. The model would be tasked with searching for plausible modifications to a past or ongoing event sequence that would result in a significantly lower predicted future risk, thereby identifying actionable intervention points (like inserting, removing, or re-ordering events) to actively design a safer trajectory, rather than passively assessing a given one.

ideas.title: Confounding-Robust Causal Intervention with Temporal Bounds
ideas.description: This system estimates how changing event sequences alters future risk while explicitly accounting for unobserved confounding. It combines temporal modeling with causal discovery to propose plausible causal structures from observational sequences, then uses sensitivity analysis to quantify the robustness of each intervention estimate. The output includes both the predicted effect of an intervention and the 'confounding strength' needed to invalidate that conclusion, enabling decision-makers to distinguish robust recommendations from fragile ones. This creates practically useful intervention guidance that acknowledges and bounds its own uncertainty.

ideas.title: Causally-Validated Protective Intervention Discovery
ideas.description: This research system identifies event sequences that genuinely cause the delay or prevention of an adverse outcome, moving beyond mere correlation. It employs causal discovery frameworks to filter candidate patterns, distinguishing direct drivers from spurious associations. The core innovation is a validation loop that tests proposed interventions in simulated counterfactual environments to estimate their real-world causal effect. The final output is a set of robust, actionable protective policies with quantified confidence in their causal efficacy.

ideas.title: Federated Pretraining of MOTOR via Swarm Intelligence and Gradient Compression
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of federated pretraining for the MOTOR foundation model by drawing an analogy from distributed computing and swarm robotics. The analogous problem of synchronizing massive, decentralized computations (e.g., in climate modeling) without crippling network congestion is solved using gradient compression (e.g., Top-k sparsification with error feedback) and decentralized coordination protocols that mimic swarm intelligence. Adapted to the healthcare context, the proposed research will investigate applying these swarm-inspired communication strategies and compression techniques to the federated pretraining process. The goal is to design a training framework that drastically reduces the communication cost and bandwidth requirements between participating hospitals or research sites, enabling efficient, scalable, and privacy-preserving pretraining of large foundation models on distributed, non-IID medical data without sharing raw patient records.

ideas.title: A Unified Pre-training Framework for Multimodal Medical Data Using a Common Latent Space
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from multi-messenger astrophysics to the challenge of scaling up pretraining on vast, multimodal medical datasets (EHR, genomics, imaging). In astrophysics, disparate signals from events like neutron star mergers are integrated by mapping them onto a shared physical model. The adapted idea proposes to pretrain a model by mapping each medical data modality into a common, semantically aligned latent space using cross-modal attention and contrastive learning objectives. This unified representation framework, which treats each modality as a different 'messenger' of the same underlying patient state, allows for more efficient and coherent scaling on massive datasets, enabling the discovery of complex, cross-modal temporal and phenotypic relationships that are inaccessible when modalities are processed in isolation.

ideas.title: Multivariate Competing Risks via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the single-event prediction focus by proposing to model the joint distribution of multiple competing or correlated time-to-event outcomes. The analogy is drawn from Ecology and Species Distribution Modeling, where ecologists use joint species distribution models (JSDMs) to model the co-occurrence of multiple species by accounting for biotic interactions and shared environmental drivers through a multivariate latent structure. Adapting this solution, the research would develop a multivariate competing risks framework that treats different event types analogously to different species. A joint model would be constructed to estimate the hazard functions for all events simultaneously, using a shared latent variable (or correlated frailties) to capture the underlying, unobserved susceptibility that influences all event risks, and a covariance structure to model the residual interdependence between specific event pairs, moving beyond the standard assumption of conditional independence given covariates.

ideas.title: Identify Protective Sequence Motifs via Evolutionary Escape Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from virology and vaccine design to solve the inverse problem of finding protective sequences. In virology, conserved protein regions critical for viral function are identified by analyzing which mutations the virus cannot easily make (escape mutants) without losing fitness; these are ideal therapeutic targets. Adapted to this context, the idea is to identify 'evolutionarily constrained protective motifs' within temporal or logical event sequences preceding a target adverse outcome. By using algorithms similar to those for predicting viral escape (e.g., analyzing mutational fitness landscapes or sequence entropy), we search for critical subsequences or patterns where intervention (prevention, alteration, or disruption) imposes the highest functional or systemic cost on the progression toward the adverse outcome. The protective sequences are those that, when secured or altered, maximally delay or prevent the outcome because the adverse process is inherently constrained to rely on them, making it difficult to 'evolve around' the intervention. This approach inverts the escape analysis to find points of maximum leverage for delay.
